injuries.name: Abdominal Muscle Strain
injuries.overviewHeading: What is an Abdominal Muscle Strain?
injuries.overview: A stretch or tear of the muscles in the stomach area, often from overuse. It’s the same as a "pulled stomach muscle" and is common in sports with lots of reaching and twisting.
symptoms: Sharp pain when you cough, sneeze, or laugh
Pain when sprinting or exercising hard
Stiffness or pain when standing up after sitting for a long time
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does an Abdominal Muscle Strain feel like?
feelsLike: A sudden, sharp pain in your stomach area during activity
Soreness that makes it hard to twist or bend your trunk
Aching in your abs when you get up from a chair or bed
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of an Abdominal Muscle Strain?
causes: Repetitive reaching and twisting, like in tennis or football
Sudden twisting movements with poor form
selfCheck: Do you feel a sharp pain in your stomach when you laugh, cough, or sneeze?
Did the pain start during or after a workout that involved twisting or sprinting?
Is the area tender, stiff, or possibly swollen to the touch?
whatToDo: Rest and take a break from the activities that cause pain
Apply an ice pack and warm compresses, alternating between them
Wear an abdominal brace to support your stomach muscles
redFlags: Pain that makes daily activities or sleeping difficult
Signs of a hernia, like a new lump or bulge in your groin or abdomen that aches or burns
Any symptoms beyond muscle main such as constipation, nausea, or vomiting
recoveryTips: Allow enough time to fully rest and heal before returning to your sport
Strengthen your core with exercises like planks to prevent future pulls
Add regular stretching, like yoga or Pilates, into your routine
Always warm up well before intense activity and cool down afterward

injuries.name: Side Stitch Syndrome
injuries.overview: Side stitch syndrome is a sharp, temporary pain in the side of your torso that happens during exercise, often called a "stitch" in your side. It's very common, especially in sports like running that involve a lot of movement in your torso.
symptoms: Sharp or stabbing pain, usually in your side
Pain most often along the lower ribs
Pain that is in one specific spot, not all over the belly
A cramping or aching feeling when the pain is less intense
Shoulder tip pain on the same side as the stitch
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does Side Stitch Syndrome feel like?
feelsLike: A sharp, stabbing feeling under your ribs when it's really bad
A dull cramp or pulling sensation when it’s milder
A specific pain you can point to, usually on the right side
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of Side Stitch Syndrome?
causes: Activities with repeated body movement, like running or horse riding
Exercising with a full stomach from eating or drinking too close to activity
Drinking sugary or flavorful drinks before or during exercise
Poor posture, especially a rounded upper back
selfCheck: Did you eat or drink a large amount less than 2 hours before exercising?
Does the pain get worse when you keep bouncing or twisting?
Is the pain in one spot along your lower ribs or side of your belly?
whatToDo: Slow down or take a break from the activity
Bend forward at the waist
Press firmly on the painful area with your hand
Practice deep, controlled breathing
redFlags: Pain that does not go away shortly after you stop exercising
Pain that is not in one spot and spreads across the belly
Pain that stops you from being able to participate in sports regularly
recoveryTips: To lower chance of getting a side stitch:
Wait 2 to 3 hours after a big meal before exercising
Avoid excessive amounts of sugary drinks right before and during activity
Staying hydrated throughout the day is key, but don’t drink too much right before exercise
Strengthen your core muscles to help support your belly
Work on improving your posture while running and sitting

injuries.name: Sports Hernia
injuries.overview: A sports hernia, also called athletic pubalgia, is a tear or strain in the muscles or tendons of the lower belly or groin that causes long-lasting pain. This injury is not a true hernia and is often from forceful twisting of the lower belly.
symptoms: Ongoing dull or burning pain in the lower belly or groin
Pain that gets worse with sprinting, kicking, or twisting
Groin pain when coughing or sneezing
Pain that improves with rest but returns with activity
Widespread pain in the affected area
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a Sports Hernia feel like?
feelsLike: Sudden, intense pain at the moment of injury
A dull or burning ache that does not go away
Pain spreading down toward the inner thigh (or near scrotum for males)
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of a Sports Hernia?
causes: Forceful and repeated twisting, kicking, or cutting motions
Weakness in the core or hip muscles
Difference between your hip and core muscle strengths
Unsafe or intense ab and hip exercises
selfCheck: Is your groin or lower belly pain made worse by sprinting or twisting?
Does the pain go away with rest but come back the moment you play?
Do you feel groin pain when you cough or sneeze?
whatToDo: Sports hernias do not generally heal on their own, so you’ll likely need medical treatment
Stop activities that trigger pain until cleared to return
Rest and ice the area for the first 7 to 10 days
After two weeks, start physical therapy focused on core and hip strength
redFlags: Pain that does not improve after several months of non-surgical care
Inability to perform your sport at all due to the pain
recoveryTips: Complete a full physical therapy program for 6 to 8 weeks
Wait 6 to 12 weeks after any surgery before returning to sport
Gradually rebuild core and hip strength to prevent re-injury
Follow a prevention program if your sport involves lots of twisting

injuries.name: Acute Abdomen
injuries.overview: An acute abdomen involves sudden, severe stomach pain that often signals a serious medical problem. It can mean an emergency that needs immediate treatment, possibly even surgery. Not all cases require surgery, but it always needs to be checked by a doctor right away.
symptoms: Symptoms of shock: Fast heartbeat, low blood pressure, sweating, or confusion
Symptoms of peritonitis: Constant pain or pain that gets worse when you touch your abdomen
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does an Acute Abdomen feel like?
feelsLike: Severe pain that starts quickly in your belly
Constant pain or pain that worsens with even a gentle bump
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of an Acute Abdomen?
causes: Inflammation or infection in the abdomen
Bleeding or blood flow blockages inside the body
Blockages or holes that form in the digestive system
selfCheck: Is your stomach pain sudden and severe?
Does your stomach area look swollen or feel very tender to the touch?
Do you feel dizzy, confused, or like your heart is racing?
whatToDo: Do not ignore sudden and severe stomach pain
Call 911 or go to the nearest emergency room immediately
redFlags: Symptoms of shock (rapid heartbeat, confusion, low blood pressure)
If untreated, this can lead to life-threatening issues
recoveryTips: Recovery completely depends on what caused the problem
Follow all instructions from your medical team after your emergency treatment
Stick to a healthy lifestyle to support your overall health
Attend all follow-up appointments

injuries.name: Jammed Finger / Finger Sprain
injuries.overview: A finger sprain, or jammed finger, is stretch or tear in the parts of your finger that hold the bones together.
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a finger sprain feel like?
feelsLike: Difficulty bending the finger
Pain when bending the finger
Finger feels stiff
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of a finger sprain?
causes: Jamming your finger into a ball or other surface
Bending your finger the wrong way
selfCheck: Does it hurt to bend your finger?
Is the joint a different color than usual (like red, purple, or darker than usual)?
Does the joint look swollen?
whatToDo: Rest and avoid activities where you could jam the finger again
Ice the finger for 15 minutes (put a cloth or towel between the ice and your skin)
Apply a compression bandage
Take anti-inflammatory drugs/NSAIDs (please talk to a parent/guardian/doctor before taking any medication)
Seek medical care if there is no improvement within a few days
redFlags: Complete inability to move the finger
recoveryTips: Manage inflammation with ice/compression/NSAIDs/elevation (please talk to a parent/guardian/doctor before taking any medication)
Rest

injuries.name: Finger Dislocation
injuries.overview: A dislocated finger happens when the finger joint bones are forced out of place. This injury can also damage the blood vessels, ligaments, tendons, and muscles that surround the joint. Dislocations can affect any joint in the body and if they are left untreated complications such as reduced function, joint stiffness, pain and swelling can continue long-term.
symptoms: Inability to move part or all of the finger
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a finger dislocation feel like?
feelsLike: Joint feels weak or unstable
Tingling or numbness in the affected finger
Pain that worsens when you attempt to move the finger
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of a finger dislocation?
causes: Quick hand movements involving contact with a ball
A previous injury to the finger joint can weaken it and make future dislocations more likely
selfCheck: Is your finger joint swelling?
Is the movement in your finger restricted?
Is your finger visibly deformed from its usual shape?
whatToDo: Visit the emergency room if you suspect your finger is dislocated
redFlags: Finger is cold to the touch
Complete loss of feeling in the finger
recoveryTips: Seek medical attention as soon as possible after the injury to avoid long term damage
Never attempt to force a joint back into place at home
Avoid putting stress on the affected joint while it heals
After the injury heals prevent future dislocations by doing exercises to strengthen the muscles around the joint

injuries.name: Finger Fracture
injuries.overview: A finger fracture, or broken finger, is when one of your finger or thumb bones breaks.
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a finger fracture feel like?
feelsLike: Difficulty bending the finger
Pain when bending the finger
Finger feels stiff
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of a finger fracture?
causes: Getting hit in the finger by a ball or other object
selfCheck: Does your finger look deformed or is it pointing in the wrong direction?
Is your finger swollen?
Is your finger a different color than usual?
whatToDo: Seek treatment from a healthcare professional
recoveryTips: Avoid painful movements or activities
See a healthcare provider as soon as you can

injuries.name: Wrist Sprain
injuries.overview: A wrist sprain is a type of injury where the parts of your body that hold your wrist bones together stretch or tear.
symptoms: Pain moving or twisting the wrist
Bruising or changes in skin color
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a wrist sprain feel like?
feelsLike: Sharp stabbing pain when you move or use your wrist
Dull aching pain while at rest, heat or tenderness of the wrist
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of a wrist sprain?
causes: Falling and landing on outstretched hands, repetitive wrist use (playing racket sports, boxing, etc)
selfCheck: Is your wrist swelling?
Does your wrist feel tender or warm to the touch?
Is movement of your wrist followed by sharp pain?
whatToDo: Avoid activities that can put stress on your wrist
Put ice packs covered by a thin towel on your wrist for 15 minutes at a time a couple of times a day
Wrap your wrist with a compression bandage but not too tightly so you don't cut off circulation
redFlags: Feeling pins and needles in your hands or fingers or you can't feel your hands or fingers at all
Can't move wrist or hand at all
recoveryTips: After initial swelling begin with gentle pain-free wrist movements
Consider over the counter pain medications to help with pain and inflammation

injuries.name: Buckle Fracture
injuries.overview: A buckle fracture is a break caused by pressure on a bone. The bone doesn't break, instead it bulges due to the pressure. It most commonly occurs in the radius, which is the forearm bone on the thumb side.
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a buckle fracture feel like?
feelsLike: Pain when moving the affected area
injuries.causesHeading: What are the common causes of a buckle fracture?
selfCheck: Do you have a bump on the affected area?
Does it hurt to move your wrist (or effected area)?
whatToDo: Seek medical treatment
recoveryTips: Take anti-inflammatory drugs/NSAIDs (please talk to a parent/guardian/doctor before taking any medication)
Follow advice from your doctor
If applicable, wear your splint as directed

injuries.name: Thumb / UCL Sprain
injuries.overviewHeading: What is a thumb UCL sprain?
injuries.overview: A UCL sprain, sometimes called "skier's thumb," is damage to the tissue connecting your thumb bone to your hand bone.
symptoms: Swelling at the base of the thumb
Bruising at the base of the thumb
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a thumb sprain feel like?
feelsLike: Pain around the base of your thumb
Difficulty bending your thumb
Difficulty grasping an object between your finger and thumb
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of a thumb sprain?
causes: Thumb getting forced backward or another direction
selfCheck: Is the base of your thumb swollen and/or bruised?
Is it painful to move your thumb or grasp an object?
whatToDo: Rest
Ice your thumb for 15 minutes (put a cloth or towel between the ice and your skin)
Take anti-inflammatory drugs/NSAIDs (please talk to a parent/guardian/doctor before taking any medication)
Seek medical care if there is no improvement within a few days
redFlags: Hearing a pop at the time of injury
recoveryTips: Avoid painful activities
Wear a splint for a few days to reduce motion

injuries.name: Mallet Finger
injuries.overview: Mallet finger is an injury to the soft tissue that helps you keep your finger straight.
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does mallet finger feel like?
feelsLike: Pain in your finger
Not being able to straighten your finger
injuries.causesHeading: What are the common causes of mallet finger?
causes: Jamming your finger hard into a ball or surface
selfCheck: Can you extend your finger?
Is your fingertip drooping?
whatToDo: Seek medical treatment as soon as possible
Take anti-inflammatory drugs/NSAIDs (please talk to a parent/guardian/doctor before taking any medication)
Make a temporary splint using a tongue depressor
recoveryTips: Rest
Follow advice from your doctor
Wear your splint

injuries.name: Muscle Contusion (Arm)
injuries.overview: A contusion is a muscle bruise. This happens when something hits your body hard enough to break the blood vessels in your muscle.
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a muscle contusion feel like?
feelsLike: Difficulty moving nearby joints
Feeling stiff
injuries.causesHeading: What are the common causes of a muscle contusion?
causes: A hard hit to your muscle such as a ball hitting you
selfCheck: Does it hurt to bend your elbow or wrist?
Do you see any bruising on your skin?
Do you see any swelling?
whatToDo: Rest the muscle for several days
Ice the area for 15 minutes at a time(put a cloth or towel between the ice and your skin)
Take anti-inflammatory drugs/NSAIDs (please talk to a parent/guardian/doctor before taking any medication)
redFlags: Feeling a tender mass where the contusion was
recoveryTips: Avoid activity that uses the affected muscle until pain subsides
Take anti-inflammatory drugs/NSAIDs (please talk to a parent/guardian/doctor before taking any medication)
Ice the area to reduce inflammation

injuries.name: Scaphoid Fracture
injuries.overview: A scaphoid fracture is a type of broken wrist that involves the scaphoid bone. This is one of the bones in your wrist and is located near the base of your thumb.
symptoms: Pain/bruising/swelling on the thumb side of the wrist
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a scaphoid fracture feel like?
feelsLike: Difficulty/inability to move your wrist
Pain in your wrist, especially near/below your thumb
injuries.causesHeading: What are the common causes of a scaphoid fracture?
selfCheck: Are you unable to move your wrist?
Is the pain, bruising, and/or swelling located on your wrist, right below your thumb?
whatToDo: Seek treatment  as soon as possible
Avoid moving/ further injuring your wrist
recoveryTips: Rest
Avoid painful activities
Follow advice from your doctor

injuries.name: Intercostal Muscle Strain
injuries.overviewHeading: What is an Intercostal Muscle Strain?
injuries.overview: A strain or tear of the muscles between the ribs, often caused by twisting, overuse, or sudden force.
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does an Intercostal Muscle Strain feel like?
feelsLike: Sharp pain with movement
Worse when twisting or reaching
Pain with deep breaths or coughing
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of an Intercostal Muscle Strain?
selfCheck: Does twisting your torso make it worse?
Does it hurt to take a deep breath?
Can you pinpoint pain between ribs?
whatToDo: Rest from aggravating movement
Ice 10–15 minutes
Avoid twisting or heavy activity
Gentle movement as tolerated
recoveryTips: Gradually return to movement
Avoid sudden twisting early
Strengthen core muscles
Progress activity slowly

injuries.name: Sternal Contusion
injuries.overview: A bruise to the breastbone (sternum) caused by a direct blow to the front of the chest.
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a Sternal Contusion feel like?
feelsLike: A sore or sharp pain in the center of chest
Worse with pressure or movement
Pain when taking deep breaths
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of a Sternal Contusion?
selfCheck: Is the pain directly in the center of your chest?
Does pressing on the area make it worse?
Did it start after a hit?
whatToDo: Rest from activity
Ice the area
Avoid pressure on chest
Monitor symptoms
redFlags: Pain spreading to arm or jaw
recoveryTips: Allow time for healing
Avoid repeat impact
Gradually return to activity

injuries.name: Clavicle Fracture
injuries.overview: A clavicle fracture, also called a broken collarbone, is a break in the bone connecting the shoulder to the chest. It commonly occurs in contact sports, cycling, falls, or direct impacts to the shoulder.
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a Clavicle Fracture feel like?
feelsLike: Sharp pain after impact
Shoulder or chest soreness
Pain with arm movement
Grinding feeling near the collarbone
Shoulder feels weak or unstable
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of a Clavicle Fracture?
causes: Direct impact to the chest or shoulder
selfCheck: Pain directly over the collarbone
Visible bump or uneven collarbone
Pain lifting the arm
Swelling or bruising after impact
Shoulder pain after a fall or collision
whatToDo: Stop activity immediately
Rest the arm and shoulder
Apply ice for 15–20 minutes
Use a sling if available
Avoid lifting or overhead movement
Seek medical evaluation promptly
recoveryTips: Follow sling and activity restrictions
Gradually restore shoulder mobility
Begin strengthening when cleared
Avoid returning to contact sports too early
Progress activity slowly
Stay consistent with rehab exercises

injuries.name: Rib Fracture
injuries.overview: A break or crack in one or more ribs, usually from a direct blow or fall onto the chest.
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a Rib Fracture feel like?
feelsLike: Sharp pain with breathing
Worse with movement or twisting
Feels like you can’t take a full breath
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of a Rib Fracture?
selfCheck: Does it hurt to take a deep breath?
Is the pain sharp when pressing on the ribs?
Did pain start after a hit or fall?
whatToDo: Rest and avoid contact
Ice 10–15 minutes
Take shallow breaths if needed
Seek evaluation if pain is severe
recoveryTips: Allow time for bone healing
Avoid contact until pain-free
Gradually return to activity
Practice controlled breathing

injuries.name: Costochondritis
injuries.overview: Costochondritis happens when the cartilage connecting your ribs to your breastbone becomes inflamed. This causes chest pain, especially with movement or breathing.
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does Costochondritis feel like?
feelsLike: Sharp or aching pain in the chest
Pain near the center of the chest
Pain when pressing on the area
Pain when breathing or moving
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of Costochondritis?
selfCheck: Is the pain near the center of your chest?
Does it hurt when you press on the area?
Does movement make it worse?
Does breathing increase the pain?
Did it start without a direct hit?
whatToDo: Rest from activity
Avoid movements that cause pain
Ice 10–15 minutes
Avoid heavy lifting
Stay lightly active as tolerated
recoveryTips: Gradually return to activity
Avoid overuse
Strengthen upper body muscles
Maintain good posture
Allow time for healing

injuries.name: Pneumothorax (Collapsed Lung)
injuries.overview: A condition where air enters the space around the lung, causing it to partially or fully collapse. This is a medical emergency in many cases.
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a Pneumothorax feel like?
feelsLike: Sudden sharp chest pain
Feeling like you can’t catch your breath
Tight or heavy chest
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of a Pneumothorax?
selfCheck: Did you feel sudden chest pain after impact?
Are you short of breath at rest?
Does breathing feel difficult or restricted?
whatToDo: Stop activity immediately
Seek emergency medical care
Stay calm and limit movement
Do not return to play
recoveryTips: Follow medical clearance strictly
Avoid activity until fully healed
Gradual return under supervision
Monitor breathing closely

injuries.name: Pectoralis Major Tear
injuries.overviewHeading: What is a Pectoralis Major Tear?
injuries.overview: A pectoralis major tear happens when the main chest muscle or its tendon is partially or completely torn, usually during heavy lifting or sudden force.
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a Pectoralis Major Tear feel like?
feelsLike: Sudden sharp pain in the chest
A tearing or popping feeling
Weakness when pushing or lifting
Pain with arm movement
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes for a Pectoralis Major Tear?
selfCheck: Did you feel a pop or tear during lifting?
Is the pain sudden and severe?
Do you feel weakness when pushing?
Is there bruising or swelling?
Does moving your arm cause pain?
whatToDo: Stop activity immediately
Rest the arm and chest
Ice 10–15 minutes
Avoid lifting or pushing
Seek medical evaluation
recoveryTips: Follow medical guidance
Gradually return to activity
Strengthen chest muscles
Avoid heavy lifting early
Allow time for healing

injuries.name: Pulmonary Contusion
injuries.overview: A pulmonary contusion is a bruise to the lung caused by a strong impact to the chest. This causes bleeding and fluid in the lung, which can make breathing difficult.
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a Pulmonary Contusion feel like?
feelsLike: Chest pain after a hard hit
Difficulty breathing or catching your breath
A tight or heavy feeling in the chest
Pain that gets worse with breathing
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of a Pulmonary Contusion?
selfCheck: Did the pain start after a hard hit to your chest?
Are you having trouble breathing?
Does breathing make the pain worse?
Do you feel tightness in your chest?
Did symptoms appear after an injury?
whatToDo: Stop activity immediately
Rest and avoid movement
Avoid deep or forceful breathing
Seek immediate medical evaluation
Stay calm and limit movement
recoveryTips: Follow medical guidance
Gradually return to activity
Avoid contact sports until cleared
Allow time for lung healing
Monitor breathing symptoms

injuries.name: Tietze Syndrome
injuries.overview: Tietze syndrome happens when the cartilage connecting your ribs to your breastbone becomes inflamed and swollen. This causes chest pain along with visible swelling.
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does Tietze Syndrome feel like?
feelsLike: Pain near the center of the chest
A sore or aching feeling
Pain when touching the area
Pain with breathing or movement
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of Tietze Syndrome?
selfCheck: Is the pain near the center of your chest?
Is there visible swelling in the area?
Does it hurt when you press on it?
Does movement make it worse?
Did it start without a direct hit?
whatToDo: Rest from activity
Avoid movements that cause pain
Ice 10–15 minutes
Avoid heavy lifting
Stay lightly active as tolerated
redFlags: Pain spreading to arm or jaw
recoveryTips: Gradually return to activity
Avoid overuse
Strengthen upper body muscles
Maintain good posture
Allow time for healing

injuries.name: Posterior Tibial Tendonitis
injuries.overview: Posterior Tibial Tendonitis is inflammation of the posterior tibial tendon, which is the tendon that runs along the arch of your foot. Inflammation in a tendon can be temporary or long-lasting (chronic). Over time, if condition is not treated, the tendon can become weakened and may break down, leading to a more severe condition known as posterior tibial tendon dysfunction (PTTD).
symptoms: Pain along the arch of the foot or the inside of the foot or ankle that starts gradually
Tenderness to touch along the inside or the arch of the foot or in the ankle
Swelling along the path of the tendon, especially in the inner ankle
Weakness in the foot or ankle, especially when "pushing off"
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does Posterior Tibial Tendonitis feel like?
feelsLike: Aching pain along the inner foot or ankle that starts gradually
Pain gets worse during activity like jumping, running, or walking/standing for long periods of time
Feeling weakness in the arch of the foot
Stiffness
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of Posterior Tibial Tendonitis?
causes: Repetitive strain injuries caused by activites such as working out too hard without warming up or cooling down or sports that require using the same motion repeatedly
selfCheck: Is there aching pain along the arch of the foot, inner foot, or ankle?
Is there weakness in the foot or ankle?
whatToDo: Rest and decrease repetitive activities such as running and jumping
Ice the affected area for short intervals
Elevate affected foot
For more severe cases, wearing a cast or boot may be required
redFlags: Visible or noticable flattening of the foot
recoveryTips: Avoiding repetitive motions during the time of recovery prevents further damage to the tendon
Extended periods of standing or walking can strain the inflamed tendon, so minimize as much as possible
Apply a cold compress to the affected areas for up to 20 minutes a day for the first few days
Elevate affected foot above the heart to reduce swelling

injuries.name: Posterior Tibial Tendon Dysfunction
injuries.overviewHeading: What is Posterior Tibial Tendon Dysfunction?
injuries.overview: Posterior Tibial Tendon Dysfunction (PTTD) occurs when long-lasting inflammation of the tibial tendon, the tendon that runs along the arch of the foot, causes the tendon to break down. As it weakens, it can no longer support the foot causing it to flatten and the ankle to turn inward.
symptoms: Pain along the arch of the foot or the inside of the foot or ankle that starts gradually
Tenderness to touch along the inside or the arch of the foot or in the ankle
Swelling along the path of the tendon, especially in the inner ankle
Weakness in the foot or ankle, especially when "pushing off"
The heel or/and toes turn outward
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does Posterior Tibial Tendon Dysfunction feel like?
feelsLike: Aching pain along the inner foot or ankle that starts gradually and worsens over time
Pain gets worse during activity like jumping, running, or walking/standing for long periods of time
Feeling weakness in the arch of the foot, especially when walking or standing
Stiffness
Walking may feel awkward or unstable
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of Posterior Tibial Tendon Dysfunction?
causes: Repetitive strain injuries caused by activites such as working out too hard without warming up or cooling down or sports that require using the same motion repeatedly
selfCheck: Is there pain along the arch of the foot, inner foot, or ankle?
Is there weakness in the foot or ankle?
Does the foot appear flat?
When walking, does the ankle turn inward?
whatToDo: Rest and decrease repetitive activities such as running and jumping
Ice the affected area for short intervals
Elevate affected foot
For more severe cases, wearing a cast or boot may be required
redFlags: Difficulty or inability to stand on tiptoe on the affected foot
recoveryTips: Avoiding repetitive motions during the time of recovery prevents further damage to the tendon
Extended periods of standing or walking can strain the inflamed tendon, so minimize as much as possible
Performing exercises to help strengthen the tendon and its supporting structures

injuries.name: Adductor Strain
injuries.overview: An injury to one of the muscles along the inner thigh that goes from the pelvic bone down to the knee, helping pull your leg inward and keep your body steady. A strain can be a slight overstretch, a partial tear, or rarely a complete tear.
symptoms: Pain or tenderness along the inner thigh or groin
Bruising that may appear within two days
A popping feeling or sound at the moment of injury
Pain that gets worse with activity and may cause limping
Muscle tightness or spasms in the groin area
Pain when bringing your legs together or pushing them apart
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does an Adductor Strain feel like?
feelsLike: A sudden sharp pull or pop in the groin during a quick movement
Aching or tightness along the inner thigh when walking or squeezing your legs together
Worsening pain when you try to run or change direction
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of an Adductor Strain?
causes: Sports with repeated kicking or fast direction changes like soccer or football
Activities needing sudden sprints or jumping like hockey, gymnastics, or track
Not warming up or stretching enough before practice
Lack of strength and flexibility training
Previous injuries to the thigh, knee, or pelvis
selfCheck: Did you feel a sudden pop in your groin during a sprint, kick, or quick turn?
Does it hurt to bring your legs together or spread them apart?
Is there tenderness or swelling along your inner thigh?
whatToDo: Apply ice for 10 to 15 minutes every 2 to 3 hours to bring down swelling
Elevate your leg above heart level when possible
Use an elastic bandage or compression sleeve for support
Rest from activities that worsen the pain, and consider crutches if limping is bad
redFlags: Symptoms that get worse or don’t improve after two weeks of care
New or unexplained symptoms developing during recovery
recoveryTips: Work with a physical therapist or athletic trainer on strengthening and flexibility exercises
Use heat for 10 to 15 minutes before gentle stretching once the initial swelling is gone
Fully complete your rehab program before returning to your sport
Maintain good overall conditioning and warm up properly to prevent reinjury

injuries.name: Hip Flexor Strain
injuries.overviewHeading: What is a Hip Flexor Strain?
injuries.overview: A hip flexor strain is an injury where the muscles connecting your thigh to your hip get stretched or torn due to your muscle being pulled beyond its limit.
symptoms: Pain in the front of your hip
A pulling or tight feeling in your hip
Weakness in your hip or lower stomach area
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a Hip Flexor Strain feel like?
feelsLike: Sudden, sharp pain during activity like sprinting or kicking
A dull ache when resting
Tightness that makes it hard to move your leg freely
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of a Hip Flexor Strain?
causes: Not warming up before practice or games
Repeating the same motion too much, like running or kicking
A sudden fall or sharp movement to the area
selfCheck: Do you have pain lifting your knee toward your chest?
Is it hard to walk without a limp?
Do you feel tightness in the front of your hip when you stand up?
whatToDo: Rest and stop the activity that caused the pain
Ice the area for 10-15 minutes, wrapping the ice pack in a towel
Wear a compression bandage or compression shorts
Elevate your hip, using pillows to prop your leg up
redFlags: You can’t move your leg at all
recoveryTips: Wait until you are pain-free before returning to sports
Gradually increase activity to avoid re-injury
Warm up and stretch properly before activity
Work on improving your overall flexibility

injuries.name: Hip Labral Tear
injuries.overviewHeading: What is a Hip Labral Tear?
injuries.overview: A hip labral tear is damage to the ring of cartilage that lines the outside rim of your hip socket. This cartilage protects your joint and helps hold the top of your thigh bone, called the femur, securely in place.
symptoms: Pain in the hip or groin area
A locking, clicking, or catching feeling in the hip
Stiffness or less ability to move the hip freely
A feeling that the hip is unsteady or might give out
Pain that gets worse with long periods of standing, sitting, or walking
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a Hip Labral Tear feel like?
feelsLike: A constant dull ache deep in the hip
Sharp, stabbing pain with certain movements, like exercise or lying on the injured side
Pain that spreads to the throughout the hip, lower back, or leg
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of a Hip Labral Tear?
causes: A sudden injury, like a fall or collision
Repetitive motions of twisting or pivoting
A natural bone shape where the hip joint doesn't fit together perfectly, causing pinching over time
selfCheck: Do you feel a deep pain in your hip or groin, especially during or after sports?
Does your hip click, catch, or lock up when you move it?
Does the pain get worse after sitting, standing, or walking for a long time?
whatToDo: Rest and take a break from activities that cause hip pain
Consider physical therapy to strengthen and stretch the muscles around the hip
Talk to a healthcare provider if symptoms don't improve with rest in a few weeks
redFlags: Severe pain in the hip or groin
You can't move your hip or put any weight on your leg
Symptoms get worse or don't improve after several weeks
recoveryTips: Strengthen the muscles around your hip as recommended by a physical therapist
Slowly return to sports only after you are pain-free and have full movement
Always warm up before and cool down after physical activity
Don't play through hip pain during or after exercise

injuries.name: Greater Trochanteric Pain Syndrome
injuries.overviewHeading: What is Greater Trochanteric Pain Syndrome?
injuries.overview: Greater Trochanteric Pain Syndrome (GTPS) is pain on the outside of the hip when the tendons and other tissues that attach to the bony notch on the outer hip become irritated.
symptoms: Pain on the outside of the hip that may spread down the outer thigh
Sharp or intense pain at first, which can turn into a dull ache
Pain that gets worse when lying on the injured side
Difficulty walking, sometimes with a limp
A catching or clicking sensation when moving the hip
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does Greater Trochanteric Pain Syndrome feel like?
feelsLike: A sharp, intense pain on the outside of the hip, especially when first standing after sitting
A deep ache that lingers on the side of the hip and thigh
Tenderness when pressing on the bony part of the outer hip
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of Greater Trochanteric Pain Syndrome?
causes: Overuse or stress on the hip from training or standing for long periods
A sudden increase in your activity level or training intensity
Running on hard or uneven surfaces
Muscle imbalances that put extra stress on the hip area
Poor foot posture, like flat feet, or wearing worn-out shoes
selfCheck: Do you feel pain on the outside of your hip when getting out of a chair or bed?
Does it hurt to lie on that side, or even on the opposite side?
Is it painful to stand on one leg?
whatToDo: Use relative rest by cutting back on activities that cause pain, like running or jumping
Apply an ice pack to the outer hip for about 20 minutes, three times a day
Wear supportive, well-cushioned shoes with good lacing
Ask a coach or physical therapist about exercises to strengthen your hips and core
redFlags: Hip pain caused by a serious fall or injury
You are unable to move your hip or put any weight on your leg
Your leg looks deformed, badly bruised, or is bleeding
Pain that goes down your entire leg
recoveryTips: Once the pain calms down, work on strengthening and stretching the hip and core muscles
Avoid increasing your workout distance, intensity, and time all at once
Warm up and stretch properly before practices and games, then cool down afterward
Gradually return to high-impact sports as your pain allows

injuries.name: Hip Apophysitis
injuries.overview: Hip apophysitis is inflammation of a growth plate on the hip or pelvis where a muscle tendon attaches. This injury occurs in growing athletes due to their open growth plates being more likely to get injured than closed growth plates.
symptoms: Dull pain in the groin or the front or side of the hip that worsens with activity
Tenderness and occasional swelling near the pain area
A limp during or after activity
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does Hip Apophysitis feel like?
feelsLike: A dull ache in the groin or front of the hip that gets worse with running or cutting
Sharp tenderness when pressing on a specific spot on the hip bone
Pain that makes you want to limp after practices or games
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of Hip Apophysitis?
causes: Overuse from running, sprinting, or twisting motions
Excessively tight hip and thigh muscles pulling on the growth plate
Playing through activity-related pain during growth spurts
Sudden increases in training intensity without enough rest
selfCheck: Do you have a dull pain in your groin or the front of your hip that gets worse with sports?
Is there a tender spot on your hip bone that hurts to press on?
Do you find yourself limping during or after activity?
whatToDo: Rest from the activities that cause pain until the tenderness goes away
Apply ice to the sore area for 20 minutes every few hours
Once daily activities are pain-free, begin gentle stretching of the hip flexors and core muscles
Make sure to progress slowly back into sport-specific movements when returning to sports
redFlags: Pain that does not improve after 1–2 weeks of rest
A visible limp that does not go away during or after activity
recoveryTips: Do not return to full sports until you can jog, sprint, cut, and jump without pain or a limp
Warm up properly before every practice or game
Stretch tight muscles after your workout
Never play through hip pain, as it can lead to a longer recovery

injuries.name: Iliopsoas Tendinopathy
injuries.overview: A condition affecting the tendon of the strongest hip flexor muscle where it attaches to the thigh bone, usually caused by repetitive hip bending movements leading to damage to the tendon.
symptoms: Deep aching pain in the front of the hip or groin
Pain that worsens with activity like kicking or running
Pain when lifting the knee toward the chest
Stiffness after sitting for a long time
Pain that travels down the front of the thigh
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does Iliopsoas Tendinopathy feel like?
feelsLike: A deep ache in the groin or front of the hip
Snapping or clicking with hip movement
Sharp pain with quick movements like sprinting or kicking
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of Iliopsoas Tendinopathy?
causes: Repetitive hip motion from sports like soccer, dance, track, or biking
Growth spurts that may reduce hip flexor flexibility
Many forceful movements against resistance, such as swimming
selfCheck: Do you feel a deep ache in the front of your hip or groin during activity?
Does your hip snap or click when you move it?
Does pain increase when bringing your knee to your chest?
Does sitting for a long time make the pain worse when you stand up?
whatToDo: Rest from activities that cause pain
Apply ice for 20 minutes every few hours for the first 1 to 3 days
Gently stretch the hip flexors, with stretches such as a kneeling lunge stretch
Do exercises that strengthen your hamstrings and glutes, such as glute bridges
redFlags: Pain that continues to worsen, even with rest
Not able to stand up straight fully
Symptoms lasting for months without improvement
recoveryTips: Return to sport only when you have full, pain-free movement
Warm up properly and cool down after all activities
Progress gradually from gentle stretching to strengthening exercises
Work on core and glute strength to support proper hip position
Add in low-resistance endurance exercise like biking or walking

injuries.name: Hip Osteochondritis Dissecans
injuries.overview: Hip Osteochondritis Dissecans is where a small section of cartilage separates from the bone underneath due to losing part of its blood supply. The separation causes pain and makes the hip joint move unevenly.
symptoms: Deep pain in the hip or groin that gets worse when during activity
Stiffness and trouble moving the hip as far as the other side
A catching, locking, or clicking sensation inside the hip
A feeling that the hip could give out or feels unsteady
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does Hip Osteochondritis Dissecans feel like?
feelsLike: A deep, dull ache in the hip or groin that builds up with running or jumping
A sharp catch or lock when moving the leg in certain ways
A strange sensation like something is floating or stuck inside the joint
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of Hip Osteochondritis Dissecans?
causes: Repeated pounding and stress on the hip joint from sports
A previous hip condition that disrupted blood flow to the area
A family history of weaker cartilage traits
selfCheck: Do you feel a deep ache in your hip or groin that gets worse with practices and games?
Does your hip ever catch, lock, or make a clicking sound when you move?
Are you starting to limp or finding it hard to move your hip as freely as the other one?
whatToDo: Stop activities that cause pain
See a doctor or athletic trainer if hip pain, limping, or catching lasts longer than a week
Use supports like crutches only if instructed, to keep weight off the hip
Follow a planned physical therapy program to strengthen the muscles around the joint
redFlags: Can't move the hip or put any weight through the leg
The hip locks completely and you can't get it unstuck
Pain doesn't settle down with rest and you have a noticeable limp
recoveryTips: Healing takes patience, and rest is critical in the beginning
Return to activity in steps only when cleared, staying away from high-impact moves like running and jumping at first
Stick with the physical therapy exercises to get back full strength and motion
Even after you're healed, you may need to adjust some activities to keep your hip healthy long-term

injuries.name: Hamstring Strain
injuries.overview: A hamstring injury is when you hurt the muscles in the back of your thigh. These muscles help you run, jump, and move your legs, so they get used a lot, especially in sports. Sometimes, if you run too fast, stretch too far, or move suddenly, you can pull or even tear them. There are different levels of injury: a small one means only a tiny part of the muscle is damaged and it might just feel sore. A medium one is a bigger tear, so it hurts more and makes moving your leg harder. A serious one is when the muscle is badly torn, which can cause a lot of pain and make it very difficult to use your leg normally.
symptoms: Pain on the back of your thigh
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a Hamstring Tear feel like?
feelsLike: A hamstring tear usually feels like a sudden sharp pain in the back of your thigh, almost like something “snapped” or got pulled too hard. The area can feel sore, tight, or weak, and it might hurt to walk, bend your knee, or even straighten your leg. You might also notice swelling or bruising in the back of your thigh later on. In milder cases it’s more like a strong ache, but in worse cases it can feel painful enough that you have to stop moving right away.
injuries.causesHeading: What are the common causes of a Hamstring Tear?
causes: Make sudden stops or quick direction changes
Jump or land awkwardly, slip or overextend your leg
Have low flexibility and don’t stretch or warm up before a sport
Start a new sport without the right training or equipment
selfCheck: Did I feel a sudden pain in the back of my thigh while running, jumping, or moving quickly?
Does the back of my thigh hurt when I walk, bend my knee, or stretch my leg?
Does my leg feel weak or harder to use than normal?
Is there any swelling or bruising on the back of my thigh?
Does it hurt more when I try to run, sprint, or kick?
Do I feel a tight or pulling feeling in the muscle?
whatToDo: Rest your leg and take a break from running, jumping, or sports
Put ice on the sore area for about 15–20 minutes at a time to help with pain and swelling
Use light support like a soft wrap if it helps it feel better
Move your leg gently only if it doesn’t hurt to keep it from getting stiff
When resting, keep your leg raised a little if it feels more comfortable
Do not push through pain or try to “play through it”
Go to a doctor is the pain is really bad and not getting better
redFlags: You can’t walk or put weight on your leg without strong pain
There is a lot of swelling or bruising in the back of your thigh
You felt a sudden “pop” or snapping feeling when the injury happened
The pain is very strong or keeps getting worse instead of better
recoveryTips: Rest your leg and avoid sports
Use ice 15 to 20 minutes
Move gently when it feels better
Return to sports slowly when pain is gone

injuries.name: Hamstring Tendinopathy
injuries.overview: A hamstring tendinopathy is a problem with the tendon in the back of your leg. A tendon is like a strong rope that connects your muscle to your bone. This injury usually happens near the bottom of your butt where the hamstring connects to the hip bone. It is common in runners and athletes who sprint, jump, or kick a lot. The tendon gets stressed too many times and starts to hurt.
symptoms: Pain high in the back of the leg
Pain when sitting for a long time
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a Hamstring Tendinopathy feel like?
feelsLike: Hamstring tendinopathy usually feels like a deep ache or soreness high in the back of your leg or under your butt. It may hurt when you run, climb stairs, squat, stretch, or sit for a long time. Some people feel stiff when they first start moving, especially after resting. The area may also feel weak or tender when touched.
injuries.causesHeading: What are the common causes of a Hamstring Tendinopathy?
causes: Going back to sports too fast after an injury. Sitting for long periods
Not getting enough rest between workouts
selfCheck: Do you feel pain high in the back of your leg or under your butt when running or sitting?
Does it hurt more when you sprint, jump, climb stairs, or stretch your hamstring?
Does the pain feel stiff or achy after resting or waking up?
Has the pain lasted more than a few days or kept coming back?
whatToDo: Rest from sprinting and jumping
Ice the sore area for 15–20 minutes
Do gentle stretching
Slowly strengthen your hamstrings
Wear supportive shoes
Warm up before sports
Do not push through sharp pain
Slowly return to sports
redFlags: Pain after a fall or hard injury
recoveryTips: Start slow when going back to sports
Stretch every day gently
Strengthen your legs and hips
Sleep enough
Drink water
Take rest days
Do not rush recovery
Stop if pain becomes sharp again

injuries.name: Hamstring Contusion
injuries.overview: A hamstring contusion is a bruise in the muscles on the back of your thigh. It usually happens when something hits the back of your leg hard during sports like football hockey or rugby. The muscles can get sore swollen and bruised after the hit. A mild contusion may only hurt a little but a severe one can make it hard to walk run or move your leg.
symptoms: Pain in the back of the thigh
Bruising on the back of the leg
Pain going up stairs or hills
Severe pain after getting hit in the leg
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a Hamstring contusion feel like ?
feelsLike: A Hamstring Contusion usually feels like a sudden pain in the back of your thigh right after getting hit. The area may feel sore tight weak or stiff and it can hurt to run jump kick walk uphill or bend forward. You may also notice bruising swelling or tenderness and in more serious cases it can hurt enough to make you limp.
injuries.causesHeading: What are the common causes of a Hamstring Contusion?
selfCheck: Did I get hit in the back of my thigh during sports or activity?
Does the back of my thigh hurt when I walk run jump or kick? , Is there bruising swelling or tenderness on my thigh?
Does my leg feel stiff or tight especially after resting?
Do I limp or have trouble using my leg normally?
Does it hurt when I bend forward or go upstairs?
Does the area feel weak or sore when touched?
whatToDo: Rest your leg and avoid sports
Use ice 15 to 20 minutes
Keep your leg raised when resting
Move gently if it does not hurt
Avoid hard stretching right away
Return to sports slowly when pain is gone
redFlags: You cannot walk without limping badly
Your thigh becomes very swollen or bruised
The pain is severe even while resting
You cannot move your leg normally
recoveryTips: Rest your leg and avoid contact sports
Use ice 15 to 20 minutes
Start gentle movement when pain improves
Return to sports slowly and carefully

injuries.name: Myotendinous Junction Injury
injuries.overviewHeading: What is a Myotendinous Junction Injury?
injuries.overview: A Myotendinous Junction injury is an injury where the muscle and tendon meet in the hamstring. This area can get overstretched or partly torn during sports that involve sprinting jumping kicking or sudden movements. The injury can cause pain tightness weakness and trouble moving the leg. It is common in athletes who run fast or do explosive movements.
symptoms: Sharp pain in the back of the thigh
Trouble bending forward,  Bruising on the back of the thigh
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a Myotendinous junction injury feel like?
feelsLike: A Myotendinous junction injury usually feels like a sudden sharp pain or pulling feeling in the back of the thigh. Some people feel a pop when the injury happens. The area may feel tight sore weak or stiff and it can hurt to run sprint jump bend forward or stretch the leg. Bruising swelling and tenderness may also happen after the injury.
injuries.causesHeading: What are the common causes of Myotendinous Junction Injury?
selfCheck: Did I feel sudden pain in the back of my thigh during sports?
Did I feel or hear a pop when the injury happened?
Does it hurt to sprint jump or stretch my leg?
Is there bruising or swelling on the back of my thigh?
Does my hamstring feel tight or weak?
Is it hard to bend forward or run fast?
Did the pain start suddenly during activity?
whatToDo: Rest your leg and avoid sports
Use ice 15 to 20 minutes
Move gently if pain allows
Avoid sprinting or jumping
Stretch lightly only when pain improves
Return to sports slowly
redFlags: Severe pain in the back of the thigh
A loud pop during the injury
Trouble walking or using the leg normally
recoveryTips: Rest your leg and avoid hard activity
Use ice 15 to 20 minutes
Start gentle movement slowly
Return to sports only when pain is gone

injuries.name: Biceps Femoris Rupture
injuries.overviewHeading: What is a Biceps Femoris Rupture?
injuries.overview: A Biceps Femoris rupture is a serious hamstring injury where one of the main muscles in the back of the thigh tears partly or completely. It usually happens during fast explosive sports movements like sprinting kicking or jumping. In severe cases athletes may feel a pop and be unable to continue playing
symptoms: Sudden sharp pain in the back of the thigh
Pain near the back or outside of the knee
Bruising in the back of the leg
Tightness or stiffness in the leg
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does Biceps Femoris Rupture feel like?
feelsLike: A biceps femoris rupture usually feels like a sudden sharp pain in the back of the thigh or near the back of the knee. Many athletes say it feels like they got kicked in the leg even when nobody touched them. The area may quickly become tight weak swollen or bruised and it can hurt to run bend forward jump or walk. Some people also feel a popping sensation when the injury happen
injuries.causesHeading: What are the common causes of a Biceps femoris rupture?
selfCheck: Did I feel a sudden sharp pain in the back of my thigh?
Did I hear or feel a pop during activity?
Is there bruising or swelling in my leg?
Does my leg feel weak or unstable?
Does it hurt to sprint jump or bend forward?
Am I limping after the injury?
Did the injury happen during a fast movement or sprint?
whatToDo: Rest your leg and avoid sports
Use ice 15 to 20 minutes
Move gently if pain allows
Avoid sprinting or jumping
Keep your leg raised when resting
Return to activity slowly
redFlags: If you heard a pop during the injury
If swelling or bruising is severe
If your leg feels very weak
recoveryTips: Rest your leg and avoid explosive movements
Use ice 15 to 20 minutes
Start gentle movement slowly
Return to sports only when pain is gone

injuries.name: Chronic Hamstring Fibrosis
injuries.overview: Chronic Hamstring Fibrosis injury happens when a hamstring injury doesn't heal properly and scar tissue builds up in the muscle. This scar tissue makes the muscle thicker and stiffer than normal, which can lead to ongoing pain, tightness, and less flexibility.
symptoms: Ongoing pain in the back of the thigh
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does Chronic Hamstring Fibrosis feel like?
feelsLike: Chronic hamstring fibrosis usually feels like a deep ache or tight pulling feeling in the back of the thigh. The pain may come and go but often gets worse with running sprinting stretching or long periods of activity. Many athletes notice stiffness especially after resting or waking up in the morning. The hamstring may feel weaker than before and not as flexible as the uninjured side
injuries.causesHeading: What are the common causes of Chronic Hamstring Fibrosis?
causes: Returning to sports too soon after a hamstring injury
Skipping warm ups and flexibility work
selfCheck: Have I had a hamstring injury before?
Does the pain keep coming back during sports?
Does my hamstring feel tight most days?
Is it painful when I run or sprint?
whatToDo: Rest from activities that increase pain
Use ice after sports if sore
Do gentle stretching when comfortable
Work on leg strength gradually
Warm up before exercise
redFlags: Pain keeps getting worse over time
You keep re-injuring the same hamstring
Significant weakness develops in the leg
recoveryTips: Follow a gradual return to sports
Stretch gently every day
Strengthen the hamstring regularly
Do not rush back into sprinting

injuries.name: Hamstring Adhesions
injuries.overview: A Hamstring Adhesion happens when scar tissue forms in the hamstring after an injury and sticks to nearby muscles or tissues. Instead of moving smoothly the tissues can become stuck together causing tightness pain and reduced movement. Hamstring adhesions often develop after a strain bruise or repeated hamstring injuries and may continue causing problems long after the original injury has healed.
symptoms: Tightness in the back of the thigh
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What do Hamstring Adhesions feel like ?
feelsLike: A hamstring adhesion usually feels like a deep ache tight pulling sensation or stiffness in the back of the thigh. Some athletes feel like they constantly need to stretch their hamstring but never get relief. The area may feel weak tight or restricted and running sprinting and stretching can become uncomfortable. Range of motion is often reduced because the scar tissue limits normal movement.
injuries.causesHeading: What are the common causes of Hamstring Adhesions?
causes: Returning to sports too soon after an injury
selfCheck: Have I injured this hamstring before?
Does my hamstring always feel tight even after stretching?
Do I feel pain or pulling when I run or sprint?
Is one hamstring less flexible than the other?
Does the area feel stiff after resting?
Do symptoms keep coming back during sports?
whatToDo: Rest from activities that increase pain
Use ice if the area becomes sore
Do gentle stretching
Warm up before sports
Gradually rebuild strength
Return to sports slowly
redFlags: Pain keeps getting worse over time
Repeated hamstring injuries in the same spot
recoveryTips: Stretch gently every day
Warm up before activity
Strengthen your hamstring gradually
Return to sports slowly

injuries.name: Proximal Hamstring Tendon Avulsion
injuries.overviewHeading: What is a Proximal Hamstring Tendon Avulsion
injuries.overview: A Proximal Hamstring Tendon Avulsion is a serious injury where the hamstring tendon tears away from the bone near the bottom of your butt. This usually happens during fast powerful movements like sprinting jumping or slipping.
symptoms: Sudden sharp pain under the butt or high in the back of the thigh
Bruising on the back of the leg
Tightness or stiffness in the hamstring
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a Proximal Hamstring Tendon Avulsion feel like?
feelsLike: A proximal hamstring tendon avulsion usually feels like a sudden sharp pain high in the back of the leg or under the butt. Many people say it feels like something snapped or popped. The area may quickly become weak swollen bruised or painful to sit on. Walking running bending forward or climbing stairs can become difficult and some people limp after the injury.
injuries.causesHeading: What are the common causes of a Proximal Hamstring Tendon Avulsion?
selfCheck: Did I feel a sudden pop or snap near my hamstring?
Is there pain high in the back of my leg or under my butt?
Does it hurt to sit walk run or bend forward?
Is there bruising or swelling in the back of my thigh?
Does my leg feel weak or unstable?
Am I limping after the injury?
Did the pain start suddenly during sports or fast movement?
whatToDo: Rest your leg and avoid sports
Use ice 15 to 20 minutes
Avoid sprinting or jumping
Move gently if pain allows
Keep your leg raised when resting
Return to activity slowly
redFlags: Severe bruising spreads down the leg
You felt a sudden pop with strong pain
Your leg feels very weak or gives out
recoveryTips: Rest your leg and avoid explosive sports
Use ice 15 to 20 minutes
Start gentle movement slowly
Follow rehab exercises carefully before returning to sports

injuries.name: Hamstring Syndrome
injuries.overview: Hamstring syndrome is a rare sports injury that happens when the sciatic nerve gets squeezed or irritated near the top if the hamstring muscles. The sciatic nerve is a large nerve that starts in the lower back and runs down the back of the leg. Although uncommon, this injury can happen after repeated running, sprinting, rowing, or after a previous hamstring injury causes scar tissue to form around the nerve. People with hamstring syndrome may have pain in their butt, the back of the thigh, or tingling and numbness down their leg.
symptoms: Deep pain in the buttock (especially near the "sit bone")
Pain in the back of the thigh
Numbness in the back of he thigh, calf, or foot
Burning pain that travels down the leg
Pain that gets worse during running or sprinting
Pain when sitting for long periods
Reduced flexibility or difficulty stretching the hamstring
Symptoms that feel similar to sciatica
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does hamstring syndrome feel like?
feelsLike: Hamstring syndrome can feel like a deep ache or sharp pain in your butt or in the back of your thigh. Some peolpe also feel tingling, burning, or numbness that travels down their leg because the sciatic nerve is being irritates. The pain often gets worse during exersice, especially with activities such as running, sprinting, or even stretching. Sitting for expended periods of time can also make the leg feel tight, weak, or uncomfortable to move.
injuries.causesHeading: What are the common causes of hamstring syndrome?
causes: Previous hamstring injury that leaves scar tissue behind
Repeated sprinting or running that puts stress on the hamstring
Overuse of the hamstring muscles from frequent training or competition
Scar tissue or adhesions that form near the sciatic nerve
Chronic inflammation near the top if the hamstring muscles
Poor flexibility in the hamstrings and surrounding muscles
Muscle imbalances that place extra strain on the hamstrings
Activities that involve repetitive hip bending such as rowing or cycling
Returning to sports too soon after a hamstring injury
Improper rehabilitation after a hamstring sprain
selfCheck: Do I feel pain deep in my buttock or near my sit bone?
Does the pain travel down the back of my thigh or leg?
Do I experience tingling, numbness, or a burning sensation in my leg?
Does sitting for a long time make the pain worse?
Does running, sprinting, or exercising increase my symptoms?
Have I had a hamstring injury in the past?
Does stretching my hamstring cause pain or discomfort?
Does my leg feel unusually tight or weak?
Do my symptoms feel similar to sciatica?
Have my sumptoms lasted for weeks or months without improving?
Does the pain return whenever I try to return to sports?
Do I notive discomfort when bending forward or lifting my leg?
whatToDo: Tell a parent, coach, athletic trainer, or healthcare provider about your symptoms.
Stop activities that make the pain worse, especially sprinting or intense exercise.
Rest the injured area and avoid overusing the hamstring.
Apply ice to help reduce pain and inflammation.
Avoid sitting for long periods if it increases your symptoms.
Follow any rehabilitation or stretching program reccomended by a healthcare professional.
Pay attention to any tingling, numbness, or weakness in your leg and report these symptoms to a doctor.
Do not ignore ongoing pain, especailly if it lasts for several weeks.
Gradually return to sports only when your symptoms improve.
redFlags: Severe pain that suddenly appears and does not improve with rest.
Significant weakness in the leg that makes it diffidult to walk, rin, or climb stairs.
Perristent numbness or tingling that spreads down the leg.
Loss of sensation in part of the leg or foot.
Pain that continues to worsen over time instead of improving.
Symptoms that interfere with daily activities, such as sitting, walking, or sleeping.
Loss of bowel or bladder control.
Severe lower back pain along with leg symptoms, which may indicate a spinal nerve problem rather than hamstring syndrome.
A noticeable deformity, bruising, or swelling after an injury.
recoveryTips: Rest from activities that cause pain.
Avoid sitting for long periods.
Apply ice to paintful areas for 15-20 minutes at a time to help reduce pain and inflammation.
Do gentle stretching only if it does not increase pain.
Follow a physical therapy program.
Practice good posture to reduce stress on the sciatic nerve.
Stay consistent with rehabilitation exercises.

injuries.name: Ischial Tuberosity Apophyseal Avulsion
injuries.overviewHeading: What is a Ichial Tuberosity Apophyseal Avulsion?
injuries.overview: A Ischial Tuberosity Apophyseal Avulsion is an injury where the hamstring muscles pull a piece of bone away from the "sit bone" (the bony bump at the bottom of the peelvis that you sit on, called the ischial tuberosity). This happens at the growth plate, a soft area of developing cartilage near the end of a bone where growth occurs in young people. Because the growth plate is the weakest link in the chain of muscle-tendon-bone, it gives away before the muscle or tendon does. This injury only happens in teenagers whose bones are still growing, around the ages of 10-18.
symptoms: Sudden, sharp pain in the buttock or back of the hip during forceful movement (sptinting, kicking, jumping)
A possible "pop" or snapping sensation at the moment of the injury
Difficulty walking such as limping of inability to put full weight on the injured leg
Tenderness when pressing on the "sit bone"
Pain bwhen bending the knee against resistance (i.e. trying to pull your heel toward your buttock while someone resists)
Bruising of the back of the thigh
Pain that may radiate down the back of the thigh, sometimes mimicjing sciatic neve pain
Pain when sitting on hard surfaces
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a Ischial Tuberosity Apophyseal Avulsion feel like?
feelsLike: A Ischial Tuberosity Apophyseal Avulsion typically occurs during a sudden, explosive moement. At that exact moment, most athletes feel or hear a distinct "pop" in the deep buttock area, followed immediately by a sudden, sharp, shooting pain. The pain is severe enough that the athlete cannot continue playing and pften has to stop the activity entirely on the spot. The pain occurs in the deep buttocks on the "sit bone," the bony bump you can feel when you sit on a hard surface. The pain typically radiates down the back of the thigh, making walking difficult and sitting painful.
injuries.causesHeading: What are the common causes of Ischial Tuberosity Apophyseal Avulsion?
causes: Jumping or hurdling - explosive takeoff movements
Sliding into a split and forced overstretching of the hamstrings
Rapid deceleration (i.e. suddenly slowing down frmo a sprint)
Lifting heavy weights with poor form
selfCheck: Am I between the ages of 10 and 18?
Did the pain start suddenly during a specific movement, rather than building up gradually over time?
Did I feel or hear a "pop" in my buttock area at the moment of injury?
Is the pain located deep in the buttock, right on the "sit bone", rather than in the middle of the back of the thigh?
Is it painful to sit on hard surfaces?
Am I having significant difficulty walking?
Does it hurt when I try to bend your knee against resistance?
Has the pain persisted or worsened despite rest and typical hamstring strain treatment?
Was I in a growth spurt around the time of injury?
whatToDo: Stop the activity immediately.
Apply ice to the buttock area for 15–20 minutes at a time, with a cloth barrier to protect the skin. Repeat every 1–2 hours in the first 24–48 hours.
Use crutches and avoid putting full weight on the injured leg until evaluated by a medical professional.
Do not stretch the hamstring. Unlike a simple muscle pull, stretching can pull the bone fragment further away and make the injury worse.
See a doctor and get X-rays.
Do not return to sports until cleared by a medical professional.
redFlags: Numbness, tingling, or shooting pain down the back of the leg.
Inability to walk at all or severe pain with any weight bearing.
Significant swelling or rapidly spreading bruising in the buttock or thigh.
Pain that gets worse over days rather than gradually improving.
Loss of strength and inability to bend the knee or extend the hip against even light resistance
Symptoms that persist beyond 2–3 weeks despite rest.
A hard lump developing in the buttock area weeks after the injury.
recoveryTips: Do not rush back to sports. Returning too early is the most common reason for complications, including nonunion (the bone never reattaching). The reinjury/complication risk is highest in the first few months.
Avoid sitting on hard surfaces for prolonged periods during recovery.
Maintain fitness safely withupper body exercises and swimming (if pain-free) can help maintain conditioning without stressing the injury.
Watch for warning signs during recovery. If pain plateaus or worsens after initial improvement, return to the doctor for repeat imaging to check healing progress.
Follow a phased rehabilitation program.

injuries.name: Hamstring Compartment Syndrome
injuries.overview: Hamstring Compartment Syndrome is an injury where pressure builds up inside the muscles in the back of the thigh. This pressure can reduce blood flow and cause severe pain swelling and muscle tightness.
symptoms: Severe pain in the back of the thigh
Tightness or pressure in the hamstring
Bruising in the back of the leg
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does Hamstring Compartment Syndrome feel like?
feelsLike: Hamstring compartment syndrome usually feels like severe pain and tightness in the back of the thigh that gets worse quickly. The leg may feel swollen hard or very painful to move. Some people notice numbness tingling weakness or a burning feeling in the leg. Walking running or stretching the leg may become extremely painful.
injuries.causesHeading: What are the common causes of Hamstring Compartment Syndrome
selfCheck: Does the back of my thigh feel very tight or swollen?

whatToDo: Stop sports and rest your leg
Use ice 15 to 20 minutes
Keep your leg raised while resting
Avoid hard stretching
Move gently only if pain is mild,See a doctor if symptoms get worse
redFlags: Severe pain that keeps getting worse
Numbness or loss of feeling in the leg
Trouble walking or moving the leg normally
The thigh feels very swollen hard or tight
recoveryTips: Rest your leg and avoid sports
Use ice 15 to 20 minutes
Follow doctor or physical therapy instructions
Return to activity slowly after pain is gone

injuries.name: Broken Nose
injuries.overview: A broken nose can occur from a bump or hit to the nose that causes a break or cracking of one or multiple bones within the nose, most commonly the bone at the top of your nose.
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a broken nose feel like?
feelsLike: A broken nose can be very painful and will likely cause nose bleeds. Your nose and eyes will likely be bruised and your nose may have a crooked look to it. Also, you may have difficulty breating through your nose.
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of broken noses?
causes: Bumps or hits to the nose during contact sports
selfCheck: Does touching your nose or area around nose cause pain?
Is it difficult to breath out of your nose?
Does your nose look crooked or twisted?
Is there bruising on and around your nose and eyes?
whatToDo: Remove from activity: Stop all physical activity, sports, or work immediately to prevent a second injury.
See a doctor
Breathe through mouth
Ice nose
Keep head up high while sitting and sleeping
redFlags: Persistent clear watery fluid drainage accomponead by salty taste in back of throat
recoveryTips: Ease Back In: Slowly add activities (light exercise, non-contact sports, ) that don't significantly worsen symptoms.
Listen to Your Body: If symptoms spike (headache, difficulties breathing, pain), stop the activity and rest more.
Progress Slowly: Gradually increase intensity and duration of physical and mental tasks as symptoms allow, with doctor's clearance.
Return to Play/Work: Follow strict, doctor-supervised protocols before returning to sports or demanding jobs to prevent re-injury.
If necesarry ask doctors about face and nose protective gear which can be worn during activities.

injuries.overview: A hip flexor strain happens when muscles at the front of your hip are stretched or torn during sports or sudden movement. This can make it painful to lift your leg or move your hip.

injuries.name: Sciatica
injuries.overview: Sciatica happens when the sciatic nerve in your lower back gets irritated. This causes pain that travels down one leg, often from your back to your foot.
