injuries.overview: A stiff neck is a common condition where the neck muscles become tight, sore, or difficult to move. In athletes, it often develops from muscle strain, poor posture, contact injuries, overuse, or sleeping in an awkward position.
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a stiff neck feel like?
feelsLike: Tight or locked-up neck
Sharp pain with movement
Difficulty turning the head
Neck feels sore or tense
Stiffness after activity or waking up
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of a stiff neck?
selfCheck: Trouble turning your head fully
Neck tightness after sports or lifting
Pain looking side to side
Muscle tension in the neck and shoulders
Symptoms improving with light movement
whatToDo: Rest briefly from painful activity
Apply ice or heat as tolerated
Begin gentle neck movement
Stretch lightly
Maintain good posture
Avoid sudden neck motions
recoveryTips: Stretch consistently
Improve posture
Warm up before activity
Strengthen neck and upper back muscles
Avoid overtraining
Return to activity gradually

injuries.name: Rotator Cuff Tear
injuries.overviewHeading: What is a rotator cuff tear?
injuries.overview: A rotator cuff tear is an injury to one or more of the shoulder muscles or tendons that help stabilize and move the shoulder joint. It commonly occurs from repetitive overhead motion, heavy lifting, falls, or contact injuries in sports.
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a rotator cuff tear feel like?
feelsLike: Dull ache deep in the shoulder
Sharp pain during lifting or throwing
Weak or tired arm feeling
Pain sleeping on the shoulder
Shoulder feels stiff or unstable
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of a rotator cuff tear?
selfCheck: Pain lifting your arm overhead
Weakness when lifting objects
Pain reaching behind your back
Shoulder pain during throwing or bench press
Pain that worsens after activity
whatToDo: Stop painful overhead activity
Rest the shoulder
Apply ice for 15–20 minutes at a time
Avoid heavy lifting or throwing
Begin gentle mobility if tolerated
Use anti-inflammatory medication if appropriate
Seek medical evaluation if pain or weakness persists
Start physical therapy as recommended
recoveryTips: Gradually rebuild shoulder strength
Focus on rotator cuff and shoulder stability exercises
Avoid rushing back into throwing or lifting
Maintain good posture during activity
Warm up properly before sports
Increase activity slowly as pain improves
Stay consistent with physical therapy exercises
Stop activities that cause sharp pain

injuries.name: Shoulder Bursitis
injuries.overview: Shoulder bursitis is inflammation of the bursa, a small fluid-filled sac that helps reduce friction in the shoulder joint. It commonly develops from repetitive overhead motion, overuse, or irritation during sports and lifting activities.
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does shoulder bursitis feel like?
feelsLike: Dull ache in the shoulder
Sharp pain when lifting overhead
Shoulder feels stiff or irritated
Pain when reaching or throwing
Soreness after activity
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of shoulder bursitis?
selfCheck: Pain lifting your arm overhead
Shoulder pain during sports or lifting
Tenderness on the outside of the shoulder
Pain that worsens with repetitive movement
Shoulder stiffness after activity
whatToDo: Rest the shoulder
Apply ice for 15–20 minutes
Avoid painful overhead activity
Begin gentle mobility exercises
Reduce repetitive shoulder stress
Seek evaluation if symptoms persist
recoveryTips: Gradually rebuild shoulder strength
Improve shoulder mobility
Strengthen the rotator cuff
Warm up before activity
Avoid overtraining
Return to sports gradually

injuries.name: Shoulder Dislocation
injuries.overview: A shoulder dislocation happens when the ligaments that connect the arm to the shoulder are placed under so much force that the ball at the top of the arm bone separates from the shoulder socket.
symptoms: Trouble moving the arm or shoulder
The shoulder looking out of place or shaped differently
Swelling or bruising around the shoulder
Weakness, numbness, or tingling in the arm or hand
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a shoulder dislocation feel like?
feelsLike: Sudden, very strong shoulder pain
A popping, snapping, or shifting feeling in the shoulder
The shoulder feeling loose, unstable, or “out of place”
Trouble or inability to move the arm normally
Weakness, numbness, or tingling in the arm or hand
Tightness or muscle spasms around the shoulder
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of shoulder dislocation?
causes: Falling onto the shoulder or an outstretched arm
Sports injuries, especially in contact sports like football, basketball, or wrestling
Car accidents or other strong impacts
Sudden twisting or pulling of the arm
selfCheck: Gently compare both shoulders in a mirror: does one look noticeably out of place?
Try very small movement: does it feel blocked or extremely painful?
Check for tingling, numbness, or loss of strength in the hand/arm
whatToDo: Get medical help immediately (urgent care or emergency room)
Do NOT try to pop the shoulder back in yourself
Keep the arm still and avoid moving it
redFlags: Visible changes in how the shoulder normally looks or sits in place
Numbness or tingling feeling in the arm or hand
recoveryTips: Rest your shoulder and don’t move it too much
Wear a sling if a doctor gives you one
Use ice packs (wrapped in cloth) to help with pain and swelling
Follow the doctor’s instructions carefully
Do physical therapy or gentle exercises when the doctor says it’s okay
Don’t go back to sports or heavy activity until a doctor says it’s safe

injuries.name: SLAP Tear
injuries.overview: A SLAP tear is an injury to the top part of the shoulder labrum, the cartilage ring that helps stabilize the shoulder joint. “SLAP” stands for Superior Labrum Anterior to Posterior.
symptoms: Pain with throwing or overhead movement
Pain with lifting or bench press
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a SLAP tear feel like?
feelsLike: Deep aching pain in the shoulder
Sharp pain during throwing or lifting
Clicking or catching sensation
Weak or “dead arm” feeling
Shoulder feels loose or unstable
Pain after repetitive overhead activity
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of a SLAP tear?
selfCheck: Deep pain with overhead movement
Clicking or popping
Weakness when lifting or throwing
Feeling of shoulder instability
Pain during bench press or throwing
Reduced shoulder mobility
whatToDo: Stop overhead activity
Ice the shoulder
Avoid heavy pressing/pulling
Begin gentle mobility only if pain allows
Schedule evaluation if symptoms persist
recoveryTips: Physical therapy is often first-line treatment
Focus on rotator cuff and scapular stability
Gradual throwing progression is important
Some athletes recover without surgery
Severe tears may require arthroscopic repair

injuries.name: Labral Tear
injuries.overview: A shoulder labral tear is an injury to the ring of cartilage that helps stabilize the shoulder joint. Labral tears commonly occur from repetitive overhead motion, throwing sports, heavy lifting, or shoulder dislocations.
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a labral tear feel like?
feelsLike: Deep aching pain in the shoulder
Sharp pain during throwing or lifting
Catching or clicking sensation
Weak or “dead arm” feeling
Shoulder feels loose or unstable
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of a labral tear?
causes: Falling on the shoulder or arm
selfCheck: Pain with overhead movement
Clicking or popping in the shoulder
Weakness during throwing or lifting
Feeling like the shoulder may slip out
Pain that worsens with activity
whatToDo: Stop painful activity
Rest and ice the shoulder
Avoid overhead lifting or throwing
Begin gentle mobility if tolerated
Seek medical evaluation if symptoms persist
Start physical therapy as recommended
recoveryTips: Strengthen the rotator cuff and shoulder stabilizers
Improve shoulder mobility gradually
Avoid rushing back into sports
Warm up properly before activity
Progress throwing or lifting slowly
Stay consistent with rehab exercises

injuries.name: Shoulder Subluxation
injuries.overview: A shoulder subluxation is a partial dislocation where the shoulder briefly slips out of place and then moves back into the joint. It commonly occurs during contact sports, falls, or repetitive overhead activity and can lead to ongoing shoulder instability.
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does shoulder subluxation feel like?
feelsLike: A sudden shifting sensation in the shoulder
Shoulder feels loose or unstable
Sharp pain during movement
Weak or “dead arm” feeling
Shoulder feels like it may pop out again
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes for shoulder subluxation?
selfCheck: Feeling like the shoulder partially slipped out
Pain after a hit or fall
Shoulder feels unstable during activity
Weakness with throwing or lifting
Fear of the shoulder popping out again
whatToDo: Stop activity immediately
Rest and ice the shoulder
Avoid overhead movement
Use a sling if recommended
Begin gentle mobility as pain improves
Seek medical evaluation for instability or repeated episodes
recoveryTips: Strengthen the rotator cuff and shoulder stabilizers
Progress activity slowly
Avoid returning to sports too early
Stay consistent with physical therapy
Improve shoulder mobility and control
Use proper throwing or lifting mechanics

injuries.name: Brachial Plexus Injury
injuries.overviewHeading: What is a brachial plexus injury?
injuries.overview: A brachial plexus injury occurs when the network of nerves connecting the neck to the shoulder, arm, and hand becomes stretched, compressed, or torn. In sports, these injuries commonly happen from hard collisions, sudden neck movement, or forceful shoulder impact.
symptoms: Weakness in the arm or shoulder
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a brachial plexus injury feel like?
feelsLike: Electric shock sensation down the arm
Burning pain after impact
Weak or heavy arm feeling
Tingling in the shoulder or hand
Sudden loss of strength
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of a brachial plexus injury?
selfCheck: Burning pain radiating down the arm
Tingling or numbness after contact
Weakness lifting or gripping
Symptoms after a neck or shoulder collision
Arm feels heavy or difficult to move
whatToDo: Stop activity immediately
Rest the neck and shoulder
Apply ice to painful areas
Avoid contact sports until evaluated
Monitor weakness or numbness
Seek medical evaluation if symptoms persist
recoveryTips: Gradually rebuild neck and shoulder strength
Improve posture and mobility
Avoid repeated contact too early
Stay consistent with rehab exercises
Progress activity slowly
Follow physical therapy guidance

injuries.name: Cervical Radiculopathy (Pinched Nerve)
injuries.overview: Cervical radiculopathy, often called a pinched nerve, occurs when a nerve in the neck becomes compressed or irritated. In athletes, it can develop from contact injuries, repetitive neck stress, poor posture, or herniated discs and may cause pain, numbness, or weakness that travels into the shoulder or arm.
symptoms: Weakness in the shoulder or arm
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does cervical radiculopathy (pinched nerve) feel like?
feelsLike: Sharp pain shooting down the arm
Tingling in the shoulder, arm, or hand
Weak or heavy arm feeling
Burning pain with neck movement
Neck stiffness or tightness
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of cervical radiculopathy (pinched nerve?
selfCheck: Pain traveling from the neck into the arm
Tingling or numbness in the arm or hand
Weakness during lifting or gripping
Neck pain worsened by movement
Symptoms after contact or heavy lifting
whatToDo: Reduce painful activity
Avoid sudden neck movements
Apply ice or heat as tolerated
Maintain good posture
Begin gentle neck mobility exercises
Seek medical evaluation if symptoms persist or worsen
recoveryTips: Improve neck and upper back posture
Gradually strengthen neck and shoulder muscles
Avoid repetitive neck strain
Stretch consistently
Return to sports gradually
Follow physical therapy recommendations

injuries.name: Whiplash
injuries.overview: Whiplash is a neck injury caused by a sudden back-and-forth movement of the head, commonly after car accidents, falls, or sports collisions. It can strain the muscles, ligaments, and soft tissues in the neck.
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does whiplash feel like?
feelsLike: Sore or stiff neck
Sharp pain with movement
Tightness in the shoulders
Head feels heavy
Pain turning the head
Neck fatigue or tension
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of whiplash?
causes: Sudden impact to the head or body
selfCheck: Pain after sudden neck movement or impact
Difficulty turning your head
Neck stiffness after activity
Headaches with neck pain
Pain spreading into the shoulders
whatToDo: Rest briefly after injury
Apply ice for 15–20 minutes
Begin gentle neck movement as tolerated
Avoid sudden neck motions
Maintain good posture
Seek medical evaluation if symptoms worsen
recoveryTips: Gradually restore neck mobility
Stay active with light movement
Avoid prolonged rest
Improve posture
Stretch and strengthen neck muscles
Return to sports gradually

injuries.name: Adductor Strain
injuries.overview: An Adductor Strain is an injury to the muscles on the inner part of your thigh and groin. These muscles help bring your legs together and help control movement during running jumping and changing directions. An adductor strain happens when the muscle is stretched too far or torn usually during quick movements like sprinting kicking or suddenly cutting in another direction. It is one of the most common causes of groin pain in athletes especially soccer and hockey players.
symptoms: Sudden pain in the inner thigh or groin
Pain when squeezing the legs together
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does an Adductor Strain feel like?
feelsLike: An Adductor Strain usually feels like a sudden sharp pain in the inner thigh or groin during activity. The area may feel tight weak or sore and the pain often gets worse when sprinting kicking jumping or changing directions. More severe injuries may cause swelling bruising and difficulty moving the leg normally.
injuries.causesHeading: What are the common causes of an Adductor Strain?
causes: Returning to sports too quickly after an injury
selfCheck: Did I feel sudden pain in my inner thigh or groin during sports?
Does it hurt when I sprint kick or change direction?
Do I feel weakness when bringing my legs together?
Does stretching my inner thigh increase the pain?
Is there swelling or bruising in the area?
Has my kicking power or speed decreased?
Does the area feel sore when I touch it?
whatToDo: Stop playing sports or exercising as soon as the pain begins
Use ice on the inner thigh for 15 to 20 minutes every few hours during the first 2 days
Avoid sprinting kicking cutting and other movements that increase pain
Use gentle pain free movements to prevent the hip and leg from becoming stiff
Begin light strengthening exercises for the inner thigh and core once daily activities feel comfortable
Slowly add jogging or direction changes and sport specific movements before returning to full competition
Avoid returning to sports too early because it can increase the chance of another injury
redFlags: You cannot walk or put weight on your leg normally
You have a sudden loss of strength in the inner thigh
Pain continues to get worse instead of improving
recoveryTips: Follow a gradual return to sports instead of immediately returning to full speed
Start with walking and easy stretching before progressing to jogging and sprinting
Strengthen your adductor muscles hip muscles and core to improve stability and prevent future injuries
Practice sport specific drills such as light kicking or direction changes before returning to games
Always warm up before training and maintain good flexibility in your hips and thighs
Continue strengthening exercises even after the pain is gone to lower the chance of another strain

injuries.name: Hamstring Strain
injuries.overview: A Hamstring strain is when the hamstring muscles on the back of your thigh are over stretched causing some the muscle fibers to tear apart. Depending on the severity a strain might only pull a few small fibers or it can tear right through it. This injury is typically graded on severity 1-3 with a grade 1 strain being mild, a grade 2 strain being partially torn, and a grade 3 strain when it is very severe and completely torn.
symptoms: Feeling or hearing a pop when injury occured
Pain when using or touching hamstring
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a torn hamstring feel like?
feelsLike: A Torn hamstring will likely be very painful and accompanied with feeling or hearing a pop coming from your hamstring and can cause difficulties with walking and sitting. Your hamstring will likely be bruised, swell, and will be tender. It will also feel very stiff and may cause muscle cramps. You may also feel pain in your butt, groin, and other parts of your legs.
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of torn hamstrings?
causes: Exerting to much force onto your hamstring during active movements
selfCheck: Does touching your hamstring cause pain?
Is there swelling or visible bruising on or around your hamstring?
Is it difficult to walk or sit?
Is there pain in the back of your thigh while walking or sitting?
whatToDo: Remove from activity: Stop all physical activity, sports, or work immediately to prevent a second injury, See a doctor
Do some Rest Ice Compression and Elevation
redFlags: Symptoms lasting for more than a few days.
Severe pain in leg while moving or sitting.
Inabillity or extreme weakness of ability to move leg.
Spreading of severe pain to back or lower leg.
recoveryTips: Ease Back In: Slowly add activities (light exercise, non-contact sports, ) that don't significantly worsen symptoms.
If necesarry ask doctors about leg and thigh protective gear which can be worn during activities.
Return to Play/Work: Follow strict, doctor-supervised protocols before returning to sports or demanding jobs to prevent re-injury.
Progress Slowly: Gradually increase intensity and duration of physical and mental tasks as symptoms allow, with doctor's clearance.
Listen to Your Body: If symptoms spike (bruising, swelling, pain), stop the activity and rest more.

injuries.name: Hip Flexor Strain
injuries.overviewHeading: What is a Hip Flexor Strain?
injuries.overview: A Hip flexor strain is an injury to one or more of the muscles at the front of your hip. These muscles help lift your knee, kick a ball, run, jump, and climb stairs. A strain happens when the muscles are stretched too far or work harder than they can handle, causing small or large tears. Hip flexor strains are common in athletes who sprint, kick, or quickly change directions. They can range from a mild pull to a more serious tear.
symptoms: Sharp pain in the front of the hip
A pulling or popping feeling during the injury
Tightness in the front of the hip
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a Hip Flexor Strain feel like?
feelsLike: A Hip flexor strain usually feels like a sudden sharp pain in the front of your hip or upper thigh. Some athletes feel a pulling or popping sensation when the injury happens. Afterward, the area may feel sore, tight, or weak. Running, kicking, lifting your knee, climbing stairs, or jumping often make the pain worse. More serious strains may cause bruising, swelling, and trouble walking normally.
injuries.causesHeading: What are the common causes of a Hip Flexor Strain?
causes: Overstretching the front of the hip
Doing too much training too quickly
Returning to sports before a previous injury has healed
selfCheck: Did I feel a sharp pain in the front of my hip while playing sports?
Does lifting my knee hurt?
Is running or sprinting painful?Does kicking make my pain worse?
Is it hard to climb stairs?
Does my hip feel tight or weak?
Do I have swelling or bruising?
whatToDo: Stop playing as soon as you feel the injury to avoid making it worse
Place an ice pack on the front of your hip for 15 to 20 minutes every few hours during the first two days
Walk only if it does not cause a limp and avoid sprinting, jumping, kicking, or hard workouts
Wear compression shorts if they make the area feel more supported
Begin gentle hip movements like slowly lifting and lowering your knee once the sharp pain starts to improve
Do easy stretches like a gentle kneeling hip flexor stretch only when it feels comfortable and does not increase your pain
redFlags: If you heard or felt a pop during the injury
If you cannot walk without limping
If you have a lot of swelling or bruising
If your leg feels very weak
If your pain gets worse instead of better
If your pain lasts longer than one to two weeks
recoveryTips: Warm up before every practice with light jogging, high knees, butt kicks, and leg swings
Stretch your hip flexors gently after practice once the muscle is healing
Strengthen your hips with bridges, clamshells, resistance band walks, and step-ups
Improve your core strength with planks and bird dogs to take stress off your hip muscles
Slowly increase your running speed and practice time instead of doing too much at once
Use a foam roller on your quadriceps and hip muscles after practice to reduce tightness

injuries.name: Lliotibial Band Syndrome
injuries.overview: Iliotibial band syndrome also called IT band syndrome is an overuse injury that happens when the iliotibial band becomes irritated. The IT band is a thick band of tissue that runs along the outside of your hip and thigh down to the outside of your knee. It helps keep your knee and hip stable while you walk run jump and change directions. IT band syndrome usually happens when the band becomes too tight and repeatedly rubs against the outside of the knee or hip. It is very common in runners cyclists and athletes who do a lot of repetitive leg movements.
symptoms: Aching pain on the outside of the knee
Burning feeling on the outside of the knee
Pain on the outside of the hip
Pain that gets worse during running
Pain when going up or down stairs
Tightness along the outside of the thigh
Clicking or snapping near the knee
Tenderness when touching the outside of the knee
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: Whar does Lliotibial Band Syndrome feel like?
feelsLike: IT band syndrome usually starts as an aching or burning pain on the outside of the knee. Some athletes also feel pain on the outside of their hip. At first the pain may only appear after a long practice or workout. As the injury gets worse the pain can start earlier during activity and may continue even when resting. Running downhill climbing stairs cycling and long periods of running often make the pain worse. Some athletes may feel clicking snapping or rubbing on the outside of their knee.
injuries.causesHeading: What are the common causes of Lliotibial Band Syndrome?
causes: Running long distances without enough rest
Running downhill or on slanted surfaces
Warming up too quickly or not preparing the muscles properly
selfCheck: Do I feel pain on the outside of my knee while running?
Does my knee hurt more when I go downhill or downstairs?
Does the pain get worse the longer I exercise?
Do I feel a burning or aching feeling on the outside of my knee?
Does cycling or running make my symptoms return?
Do I notice tightness along the outside of my thigh?
whatToDo: Take a break from running cycling or other activities that cause pain
Use an ice pack on the outside of your knee for 15 to 20 minutes after activity to reduce soreness
Switch to low impact exercise like swimming or easy upper body workouts while the knee heals
Use a foam roller on your glutes quadriceps and surrounding leg muscles to help reduce tightness
Try gentle stretches for your hips glutes and thighs after the painful area starts to calm down
redFlags: If your pain does not improve after several weeks
If the pain keeps coming back every time you exercise,  If walking or normal daily activities become painful
If you have significant swelling redness or warmth around your knee
If you are unsure whether you have IT band syndrome or another knee injury
recoveryTips: Warm up slowly before practice with easy jogging leg swings and light movement drills
Increase your running distance speed and training time little by little instead of making big jumps
Strengthen your hips and glutes with resistance bands bridges and single leg balance exercises
Stretch your hips and thighs after practices to improve flexibility

injuries.name: Quadricep Strain
injuries.overview: A Quadriceps strain is an injury to one of the muscles in the front of your thigh. It happens when the muscle is stretched too far or torn during activities like sprinting kicking jumping or sudden changes in direction. Quadriceps strains are common in sports and can range from a mild pull to a more serious tear.
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a Quadriceps strain feel like?
feelsLike: A Quadriceps strain usually feels like a sudden sharp pain in the front of the thigh during activity. The muscle may feel tight sore weak or stiff. Some athletes feel a popping sensation when the injury happens. Walking running kicking squatting or climbing stairs may become painful and bruising or swelling can develop later.
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of a Quadriceps strain?
selfCheck: Did I feel sudden pain in the front of my thigh?
Did the injury happen while sprinting kicking or jumping?
Does my thigh feel tight or weak?
Does it hurt to run squat or kick?
Is there bruising or swelling in my thigh?
Did I feel or hear a pop when it happened?
Is it hard to use my leg normally?
whatToDo: Rest your leg and avoid sports
Use ice 15 to 20 minutes
Move gently when pain improves
Avoid sprinting and kicking
Keep your leg raised when resting
Return to sports slowly
redFlags: You felt a pop with immediate pain
Your leg feels very weak or gives out
recoveryTips: Rest your leg and avoid sports
Use ice 15 to 20 minutes
Start gentle movement slowly
Return to sports only when pain is gone

injuries.name: Thigh Contusion
injuries.overview: A thigh contusion is a deep bruise that happens when your thigh gets hit hard by another player an object or the ground. It most often affects the quadriceps muscles on the front of your thigh which help you straighten your knee and lift your leg. A hard hit can damage the muscle and small blood vessels causing blood to collect inside the muscle. Thigh contusions can range from mild soreness to a severe injury that makes it difficult to walk or move your leg.
symptoms: Pain in the front of the thigh
A deep aching or throbbing feeling
Bruising or a dark mark on the skin
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a Thigh Contusion feel like?
feelsLike: A Thigh contusion usually feels like a deep aching or throbbing pain in the front of your thigh. The area may feel sore when you touch it and moving your knee or hip can become painful. Mild bruises may only cause discomfort while severe contusions can cause significant pain stiffness swelling and trouble walking.
injuries.causesHeading: What are the common causes of a Thigh Contusion?
causes: Getting hit by another player’s knee elbow or body
Being struck by a ball stick or piece of equipment
Crashing during sports like biking skiing or skateboarding
Not wearing proper protective equipment in contact sports
selfCheck: Did I get hit hard in my thigh during sports?
Is my thigh painful or sore to touch?
Does it hurt to walk run or bend my knee?
Is there swelling or bruising on my thigh?
Does my leg feel tight or stiff?
Am I limping when I walk?
Is it difficult to move my knee normally?
whatToDo: Stop playing right away and avoid activities that make your thigh hurt
Use an ice pack wrapped in a towel for 15 to 20 minutes several times a day during the first few days
Wrap your thigh with a compression sleeve or elastic bandage to help control swelling
Rest with your leg raised on a pillow to reduce swelling
Avoid heat massages and deep stretching during the first few days because they can increase swelling
redFlags: If you cannot put weight on your injured leg
If you cannot bend your knee past a right angle
If your thigh swells very quickly after the injury
If the pain becomes worse instead of better
If bruising and swelling continue to increase
If you cannot return to normal walking after several days
recoveryTips: Start with easy walking before returning to running
Slowly increase running speed and practice time over several days or weeks
Strengthen your quadriceps with exercises like wall sits leg raises and resistance band knee extensions
Improve flexibility with gentle quadriceps stretches once your doctor or trainer says it is safe

injuries.name: Rectus Femoris Strain
injuries.overviewHeading: What is a Rectus Femoris Strain?
injuries.overview: A Rectus Femoris Strain is an injury to the rectus femoris muscle which is one of the four quadriceps muscles on the front of your thigh. It is the quadriceps muscle most often injured because it crosses both the hip and knee joints. The injury usually happens during sprinting kicking jumping or sudden changes in direction. A rectus femoris strain can range from a mild stretch to a partial or complete tear.
symptoms: Sudden pain in the front of the thigh
Pain near the front of the hip or groin
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a Rectus femoris strain feel like?
feelsLike: A Rectus Femoris Strain usually feels like a sudden sharp pain in the front of the thigh or near the front of the hip. Some athletes feel a pulling sensation or even a pop when the injury happens. The muscle may become tight weak swollen or bruised. Running sprinting kicking jumping and stretching the leg often make the pain worse
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of Rectus Femoris Strain?
selfCheck: Did I feel sudden pain in the front of my thigh or hip?
Did the injury happen while sprinting kicking or jumping?
Does my thigh feel tight or weak?
Does it hurt to run or kick?
Is there bruising or swelling in my thigh?
Did I feel or hear a pop?
Does stretching the leg increase the pain?
whatToDo: Stop playing sports or exercising as soon as the injury happens
Use ice on the front of your thigh for 15 to 20 minutes every few hours during the first 2 days
Walk carefully and avoid sprinting kicking jumping or activities that increase pain
Try gentle knee bending and straightening movements to keep the leg from getting too stiff
Avoid aggressive stretching right away because it can make the injury worse
If walking causes a limp reduce activity and give the muscle more time to recover
redFlags: You felt a pop with immediate pain
Your leg feels very weak or gives out
recoveryTips: Warm up for at least 10 minutes before returning to sports
Start with light jogging before progressing to sprinting
Practice easy kicking drills before returning to full kicking power
Gradually increase running speed over several days instead of returning to full effort immediately
Continue gentle stretching after activity to maintain flexibility
Strengthen both legs to reduce the chance of another injury
Stop and rest if pain returns during practice or games

injuries.name: Gracilis Muscle Strain
injuries.overviewHeading: What is a Gracilis muscle strain?
injuries.overview: A Gracilis Muscle Strain is an injury to the gracilis muscle which is a long thin muscle that runs along the inside of your thigh from your pelvis down to the inside of your knee. It is part of the group of inner thigh muscles called the adductors. The gracilis helps pull your leg inward and helps with movements like running walking climbing stairs bending your knee and changing directions. A strain happens when the muscle gets stretched too far or works harder than it can handle causing small or larger tears in the muscle. This can happen suddenly during sports or slowly from doing too much activity without enough recovery
symptoms: Sharp pain in the inner thigh or groin, Pain near the inside of the knee
A pulling or popping feeling during the injury
Weakness when squeezing your legs together
Pain when cutting or changing directions
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a Gracilis Muscle Strain feel like?
feelsLike: A Gracilis Strain can feel like a sudden sharp pull or pain along the inner thigh or groin. Some athletes feel a popping feeling when the injury happens. The area may feel tight sore weak or stiff. It may hurt more when running sprinting kicking changing directions climbing stairs or even walking. More serious strains may cause bruising or swelling along the inner thigh or near the inside of the knee.
injuries.causesHeading: What are the common causes of a Gracilis Muscle Strain?
causes: Sprinting or running at high speeds
Quick side movements or changing direction suddenly
Kicking a ball with a lot of force
Slipping and forcing the legs too far apart
Doing too much training too quickly
Tight or weak hip and thigh muscles
Not getting enough rest between practices
selfCheck: Did I feel a sudden pull or sharp pain in my inner thigh during sports?
Does it hurt when I run sprint or change directions?
Is kicking a ball painful or weaker than normal?
Does squeezing my legs together cause pain or weakness?
Is my inner thigh sore when I touch it?
Do I feel tight or stiff after sitting for a while?
Is it harder to stretch my leg than before?
whatToDo: Stop playing sports right away if the pain is sharp or gets worse
Use ice for 15 to 20 minutes after the injury to help calm pain and swelling
Keep moving with easy activities like short walks because complete bed rest can make the muscle become stiffer
Avoid hard running sprinting kicking and side movements until the muscle feels stronger
Use a compression sleeve or compression shorts to support the inner thigh during daily activities
Try gentle range of motion exercises like slow leg swings or carefully moving the leg in and out without pain
After the first few days start easy strengthening exercises like squeezing a pillow between your knees
As you improve add exercises like side lying leg lifts wall squats and resistance band exercises to rebuild inner thigh strength
A foam roller can be used on the surrounding thigh muscles to reduce tightness but do not press hard directly on the painful spot
Slowly return to sports by starting with walking then jogging then sprinting and finally full speed movements
redFlags: A loud pop with sudden severe pain
The leg feels very weak or numb
recoveryTips: Warm up before every practice with light jogging side shuffles and easy leg swings
Improve flexibility with gentle butterfly stretches and other inner thigh stretches after the muscle starts feeling better
Strengthen your inner thighs using pillow squeezes resistance bands and side lying leg lifts
Build stronger hips and glutes with exercises like bridges clamshells and band walks so your inner thigh does not have to work too hard
Increase your running speed and practice time slowly instead of doing too much too soon
Take rest days between hard workouts so your muscles have time to recover
Check your sports equipment like shoes or bike setup because poor equipment can place extra stress on your inner thigh
Keep doing strength and flexibility exercises even after the pain is gone to help prevent the injury from coming back

injuries.name: Vastus Lateralis Strain
injuries.overviewHeading: What is a Vastus Lateralis strain?
injuries.overview: A Vastus Lateralis strain is an injury to the vastus lateralis muscle which is the large muscle on the outer part of the front thigh. It is one of the four quadriceps muscles and helps straighten the knee during walking running jumping and kicking. A strain happens when the muscle is stretched too far or partly torn during sports or exercise. This injury is common in athletes who sprint jump or perform explosive movements.
symptoms: Pain on the outer front part of the thigh
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a Vastus Lateralis Strain feel like?
feelsLike: A Vastus Lateralis Strain usually feels like a sudden sharp pain or pulling sensation on the outer front part of the thigh. The muscle may become tight sore weak or stiff. Running sprinting jumping squatting and climbing stairs often make the pain worse. More serious strains can lead to swelling bruising and difficulty using the leg normally
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of a Vastus Lateralis Strain?
selfCheck: Did I feel pain on the outer front part of my thigh?
Did the injury happen while sprinting jumping or squatting?
Does the muscle feel tight or weak?
Does it hurt when I run climb stairs or squat?
Is there swelling or bruising in my thigh?
Does stretching the thigh increase the pain?
whatToDo: Stop sports or exercise as soon as the injury happens
Use ice on the sore area for 15 to 20 minutes every few hours during the first 2 days
Avoid sprinting jumping squatting and activities that increase pain
Walk normally if comfortable but avoid pushing through pain
Try gentle knee bending and straightening movements to prevent stiffness
Avoid deep stretches during the first few days
redFlags: Your leg feels very weak or unstable
Pain gets worse instead of better
recoveryTips: Warm up for at least 10 minutes before activity
Begin with walking then progress to light jogging before sprinting
Increase running speed gradually over several days
Continue gentle stretching once pain decreases
Strengthen your quadriceps and hip muscles regularly
Avoid returning to full sports until you can run jump squat and climb stairs without pain

injuries.name: Adductor Longus Tendon Tear
injuries.overviewHeading: What is an Adductor longus tendon tear?
injuries.overview: An Adductor Longus Tendon Tear is an injury to a strong band of tissue that connects the inner thigh muscle to your hip bone. This muscle helps you bring your leg toward the middle of your body and helps with movements like running fast, kicking a ball, jumping, and changing directions. The injury can happen when the inner thigh is stretched too far or has to work very hard all of a sudden. Small tears may cause some pain and weakness, while bigger tears can make it hard to run, kick, or move normal.
symptoms: Sharp pain in the groin or inner thigh
A popping feeling or sound when the injury happens
Tightness or soreness in the inner thigh
Weakness when trying to squeeze your legs together
Pain when running fast or changing direction
Pain during side steps or lunges
Trouble pushing off the ground while running
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does an Adductor Longus Tendon Tear feel like?
feelsLike: An Adductor Longus Tendon Tear usually feels like a sudden sharp pain in your groin or inner thigh. Some athletes say they feel a pop or a snapping feeling when it happens. After the injury, the area may feel tight, weak, or sore. Running, kicking, cutting, side movements, and stretching the inner thigh may hurt. A more serious tear can also cause swelling, bruising, and a loss of speed and power when trying to sprint or jump.
injuries.causesHeading: What are the common causes of an Adductor Longus Tendon Tear?
causes: Sprinting or starting a run very quickly
Kicking a ball with a lot of force
Playing when your muscles are tired
Not warming up before practice or games
Going back to sports before a past groin injury has fully healed
selfCheck: Did I feel a sharp pain or pop in my inner thigh during sports?
Does it hurt when I run, kick, or move side to side?
Is it painful to squeeze my legs together?
Do I have bruising or swelling on my inner thigh?
Does my leg feel weaker than normal?
Is it harder to sprint or jump with power?, Does stretching my inner thigh hurt?
whatToDo: Stop playing right away so you do not make the tear worse
Put an ice pack on the sore area for 15 to 20 minutes every few hours during the first two days
Wear compression shorts or a stretchy wrap to help support the inner thigh and reduce swelling
Walk slowly and avoid running, kicking, jumping, or quick side movements while it hurts
If walking is painful, use crutches or ask an adult or doctor about extra support
Do easy movements like slowly moving your leg or gentle hip circles to keep your leg from getting stiff
Do not force yourself into deep stretches because this can make the tear worse
When the pain gets better, start easy strengthening exercises like squeezing a pillow between your knees or using a light resistance band
redFlags: A loud pop followed by strong pain
A lot of bruising or swelling in the inner thigh
Your leg feels very weak or gives out
recoveryTips: Start with easy exercises like walking or riding a stationary bike before running again
Slowly build back up from jogging to faster running and then sprinting
Strengthen your inner thigh using exercises like pillow squeezes, resistance bands, and easy side lunges
Build stronger hips and core with exercises like bridges, side steps with a band, and simple planks
Always warm up before sports with movements like leg swings, easy lunges, and light jogging
Practice easy kicking and side-to-side movements before going back to full games
Only return to sports when you can run, jump, cut, and kick without pain
Keep doing strength and stretching exercises even after you feel better to help prevent the injury from coming back

injuries.name: Femoral Stress Fracture
injuries.overviewHeading: What is a Femoral stress fracture?
injuries.overview: A Femoral stress fracture is a small crack in your thigh bone. It happens when the bone is put under too much stress over and over without enough time to recover. Instead of one big injury the bone slowly becomes weaker until a small crack forms. This injury is most common in runners and athletes who suddenly increase how much they train. If it is not treated early the crack can get bigger and become a complete break.
symptoms: Deep pain in the thigh or groin
Pain that gets worse during running
Pain when putting weight on the leg
Pain that gets better with rest at first
Pain that slowly gets worse over time
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a Femoral stress factor feel like?
feelsLike: A Femoral stress fracture usually starts as a tiny ache deep in your thigh or groin. At first the pain may only happen during running or sports and go away with rest. As the injury gets worse the pain can last longer and may even hurt while walking or standing. Some athletes notice they cannot run as fast or jump as well because of the pain.
injuries.causesHeading: What are the common causes of a Femoral stress factor
causes: Doing too many hard workouts without rest
Returning to sports too soon afte
selfCheck: Do I have a deep ache in my thigh or groin when I run?
Has my pain slowly gotten worse over the last few weeks?
Does the pain improve with rest but come back when I exercise?
Am I limping because of the pain?
Did I recently increase my training or mileage?
Does hopping on one leg make the pain worse?
whatToDo: Stop running jumping and other high impact sports as soon as the pain starts
Tell a parent coach or athletic trainer about your pain early before the injury gets worse
Use crutches if walking is painful or you are limping
Switch to low impact exercise like swimming or pool running if your doctor says it is okay
Eat healthy foods with enough calcium protein and vitamin D to help your bones heal
Wear supportive athletic shoes and replace old shoes that have worn out
Follow your doctor’s plan before returning to sports because running too soon can make the crack bigger
redFlags: You cannot put weight on your leg
Sudden sharp pain after weeks of aching
Your leg gives out or you cannot walk
recoveryTips: Follow your doctor’s plan and do not return to sports too early
Build back slowly by walking before jogging and jogging before sprinting, Strengthen your hips glutes and legs with exercises like bridges clamshells and bodyweight squats
Use a stationary bike or swim to stay active while your bone heals
Eat enough calcium vitamin D and protein every day to build stronger bones
Increase running distance by only a small amount each week instead of making big jumps
Replace worn out running shoes and train on softer surfaces when possible
Continue strength training even after you return to sports to lower your chance of another stress fracture

injuries.name: Sartorius Muscle Strain
injuries.overviewHeading: What is a Sartorius Muscle Strain?
injuries.overview: A Sartorius Muscle Strain is an injury to the Sartorius Muscle which is the long thin muscle that runs from the outside of your hip across the front of your thigh to the inside of your knee. It helps you lift your knee bend your knee and move your leg into different positions. A strain happens when this muscle is stretched too far or works harder than it can handle. This can happen from running sprinting jumping cycling or making quick changes in direction during sports. It can also happen when the muscle becomes tight from sitting for long periods or overuse.
symptoms: Pain in the front of the hip or front of the thigh
Pain on the inside of the knee
Pain when stretching the hip or thigh
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a Sartorius Muscle Strain feel like?
feelsLike: A Sartorius Strain can feel like a sharp pain or a burning feeling in the front of the hip down the front or inner part of the thigh or near the inside of the knee. The muscle may feel tight sore or weak. It may hurt more when you run climb stairs lift your knee kick or stretch the front of your hip and thigh. Some athletes notice that their leg feels stiff or less flexible especially after sitting or after a hard practice.
injuries.causesHeading: What are the common causes of a Sartorius Muscle Strain?
causes: Running long distances without enough rest
Sitting for a long time without stretching
Tight hip muscles causing the sartorius to work harder
Weak glutes or core muscles making the sartorius take on extra work
selfCheck: Do I have pain in the front of my hip or thigh when I run?
Does lifting my knee cause pain or weakness?
Does climbing stairs make my pain worse?
Does my hip or thigh feel tight after sitting for a long time?
Does sprinting or changing direction increase my pain?
Does stretching the front of my hip feel uncomfortable?
Does my leg feel weaker or less flexible than normal?
whatToDo: Stop activities that cause pain and avoid sprinting jumping or hard cutting movements
Use an ice pack for 15 to 20 minutes after activity if the area feels painful or irritated
Try gentle movements like easy walking or slowly bending and straightening your knee to prevent stiffness
Perform gentle hip flexor stretches and easy leg swings once the sharp pain starts to go away
Use a foam roller on the surrounding thigh muscles to reduce tightness but avoid pressing directly on a painful area
Begin light strengthening with exercises like bridges and slow step ups to build stronger hips and reduce stress on the sartorius
Use a resistance band for gentle hip exercises as pain improves
Slowly return to running by starting with walking then jogging before sprinting again
redFlags: Severe pain that does not improve with rest
The leg feels very weak or gives out
recoveryTips: Warm up with light jogging high knees and leg swings before every practice
Strengthen your glutes and core with bridges or planks and resistance band walks
Improve hip flexibility with gentle hip flexor stretches and mobility exercises
Increase running distance and speed little by little instead of all at once
Use a foam roller after practice to reduce muscle tightness
Make sure your bike fit and posture are correct if you cycle often
Take rest days between hard practices to let your muscles recover

injuries.name: Tensor Fasciae Latae strain
injuries.overviewHeading: What is a Tensor Fasciae Latae strain?
injuries.overview: A Tensor Fasciae Latae (TFL) strain is an injury to a small muscle on the outside of the front part of your hip. The TFL works together with the Iliotibial band (IT band) to help keep your pelvis and knee stable while standing walking running and balancing. It also helps lift your leg move it out to the side and rotate it inward. A strain happens when the muscle is stretched too far or works harder than it can handle causing small tears in the muscle. This injury can happen during sports especially when running on hills changing direction quickly or doing repeated hip movements.
symptoms: Pain on the outside front of the hip
Pain when climbing hills or stairs
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a Tensor Fasciae Latae strain feel like?
feelsLike: A TFL strain usually feels like a sharp or aching pain on the outside front part of your hip. The area may feel tight sore or weak. It may hurt when you run sprint climb stairs lift your knee or stand on one leg. Some athletes notice that their hip feels stiff after sitting for a long time or after a hard workout. In more serious strains there may be swelling tenderness or trouble moving normally.
injuries.causesHeading: What are the common symptoms of a Tensor Fasciae Latae strain?
causes: Running uphill or on sloped surfaces
Doing too much running without enough rest
Weak hip or glute muscles causing the TFL to work harder
Not warming up before practice or games
Repeating the same movements over and over
selfCheck: Do I have pain on the outside front of my hip?
Does it hurt more when I run sprint or go up stairs?
Does standing on one leg make the pain worse?
Do I feel tight or sore around my hip after practice?
Does lifting my knee cause pain or weakness?
Did my pain start after increasing my training?
Does my hip feel less flexible than normal?
whatToDo: Stop activities that cause sharp pain especially sprinting hills and quick side movements
Use an ice pack on the outside of your hip for 15 to 20 minutes after activity to help calm pain and swelling
Try easy movement like walking or gentle hip circles so your hip does not become too stiff
Avoid deep stretches at first because stretching it right away can make it worse
Use a foam roller on nearby muscles like your thighs and butt to reduce tightness but avoid rolling directly over a painful spot
Begin easy strengthening exercises like bridges and side leg raises once daily activities feel comfortable
Use a resistance band for lateral walks and controlled hip exercises to rebuild hip stability
Slowly return to running by starting with walking then easy jogging then faster running

If your hip feels very weak or unstable
If the pain does not improve after a few weeks
If you have pain that keeps returning every time you play sports
If you notice a lump or unusual swelling around the hip
recoveryTips: Warm up before every practice with light jogging high knees and leg swings
Improve hip mobility with gentle stretches once the pain starts going away
Increase running distance speed and hill training slowly instead of doing too much at once
Practice balance exercises like standing on one leg or using a balance pad to improve hip control
Take rest days between hard workouts so your muscles have time to recover
Do not return to full sprinting cutting or competition until you can run jump and change directions without pain

injuries.name: Quadriceps Tendon Rupture
injuries.overviewHeading: What is a Quadriceps tendon rupture?
injuries.overview: A Quadriceps tendon rupture is a serious injury where the tendon connecting the quadriceps muscles to the kneecap tears partially or completely. The quadriceps are the large muscles on the front of the thigh that help straighten the knee. This injury often happens during sports when an athlete lands from a jump changes direction suddenly or tries to stop a fall. A complete rupture can make it impossible to straighten the knee normally.
symptoms: Bruising around the knee or lower thigh
injuries.feelsLikeHeading: What does a Quadriceps Tendon Rupture feel like?
feelsLike: A Quadriceps Tendon Rupture usually feels like a sudden sharp pain just above the kneecap. Many people feel or hear a pop when the injury happens. The knee may become swollen and weak and it may be difficult or impossible to straighten the leg. Walking running climbing stairs or getting up from a chair can become very difficult
injuries.causesHeading: What are common causes of a Quadriceps Tendon Rupture?
causes: Sudden change in direction while running
Trying to catch yourself during a fall
Strong force through the knee while the quadriceps are contracting
Sports involving jumping sprinting or explosive movements

Keep your leg raised when resting
Avoid running jumping and squatting
